class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Director Steve Pink’s “The Last Republican,” which follows former Illinois Congressman Adam Kinzinger’s unwavering fight to hold President Donald Trump accountable for the January 6 insurrection, will screen at the Davis Theater on April 28.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chicago International Film Festival</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Among the films the fest will screen in the “Speak Truth” series is director Steve Pink’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/movies-tv/2024/10/31/adam-kinzinger-the-last-republican-documentary-steve-pink-streaming" target="_blank" ><u>“The Last Republican,”</u></a> which follows former Illinois Congressman Adam Kinzinger’s unwavering fight to hold President Donald Trump accountable for the January 6 insurrection. The screening on April 28 will include Kinzinger, via Zoom, and a panel discussion about fair elections and how everyday people can get involved in politics.</p><p>Chicago Media Project co-founder Steven Cohen said the idea this year is to flip the event from being just a film festival into also being an ideas festival. “Film is the galvanizing point and it's the reason people come together. But we go beyond there. We want Doc10 to not be the place where film is the end of the conversation, but the beginning of the conversation.”</p><p>Put simply, Cohen said, a running theme throughout this year's festival is “collective courage.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b20000" name="image-b20000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e086445/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+263/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff5%2F10%2F64c2e43e45059155773eb8c2553b%2Fpaula-froehle-and-steve-cohen-2025doc10-closingnight-credit-vee-sanders.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/28b5c27/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+263/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff5%2F10%2F64c2e43e45059155773eb8c2553b%2Fpaula-froehle-and-steve-cohen-2025doc10-closingnight-credit-vee-sanders.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8f5b94f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+263/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff5%2F10%2F64c2e43e45059155773eb8c2553b%2Fpaula-froehle-and-steve-cohen-2025doc10-closingnight-credit-vee-sanders.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Paula Froehle and Steve Cohen at Doc10 in 2025" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8f5b94f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+263/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff5%2F10%2F64c2e43e45059155773eb8c2553b%2Fpaula-froehle-and-steve-cohen-2025doc10-closingnight-credit-vee-sanders.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fe8d57e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+263/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff5%2F10%2F64c2e43e45059155773eb8c2553b%2Fpaula-froehle-and-steve-cohen-2025doc10-closingnight-credit-vee-sanders.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8f5b94f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+263/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff5%2F10%2F64c2e43e45059155773eb8c2553b%2Fpaula-froehle-and-steve-cohen-2025doc10-closingnight-credit-vee-sanders.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“Film is the galvanizing point and it’s the reason people come together. But we go beyond there. We want Doc10 to not be the place where film is the end of the conversation, but the beginning of the conversation,” Doc10 Co-founder Steve Cohen (right) said. Here, he stands with fellow Doc10 co-founder Paula Froehle in 2025.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Vee Sanders</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The line up of “Speak Truth” films also includes a slate of shorts with strong local ties. “ICE Under Watch,” screening May 3, is a collection of films by Chicago-based filmmakers that discuss immigration with a specific focus on Operation “Midway Blitz,” the monthslong federal immigration enforcement action that unfolded across the Chicago area last fall.</p><p>One of the docs in this series, “El Sueño,” is unfinished, but will screen a 10-minute excerpt. The project started with filmmaker Carlos Javier Ortiz filming Venezuelan refugees that were sent to Chicago in 2022 by Texas Governor Greg Abbott. Then, when Trump was reelected, Ortiz documented the rapid changes in immigration policy, leading up to events of Operation Midway Blitz.</p><p>“I think for us, an important aspect of this film is that this is going to be Chicago’s historic memory,” said Alexandra Halkin, “El Sueño” producer. I think everybody that has been living in Chicago and dealing with the situation of [Immigration and Customs Enforcement] and border patrol, we all live through it one way or the other.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe src="https://player.vimeo.com/video/1147204272?app_id=122963" width="426" height="240" frameborder="0" allow="autoplay; fullscreen; picture-in-picture; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" title="Trailer El Sueño-ORTIZmp4"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>Halkin said the four-year chunk of time the film covers starts with <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/race-class-communities/2022/08/31/texas-buses-dozens-of-migrants-to-chicago" target="_blank" ><u>the arrival of busloads</u></a> of Venezuelans sent from the border to Chicago in 2022 and ends with the 2026 midterm elections — a four-year chunk of “collective trauma.”</p><p>The ensuing discussion will focus on the power of media, collective organizing and solidarity in times of heightened enforcement and resistance.</p><p>This year’s official Doc10 selections serendipitously align with some of the themes from “Speak Truth,” even though senior programmer Anthony Kaufman said films are selected strictly based on merit. “It's finding the best films that we can, and then seeing what kind of themes emerge organically from the films themselves.”</p><p>Even so, the coveted 10 slots in the group of official selections contain films like “Everybody to Kenmure Street,” by award winning filmmaker Felipe Bustos Sierra. Set in the Scottish city of Glasgow, the documentary unfolds on a morning in May 2021, when immigration enforcement officers attempted to detain two community members.</p><p>The documentary uses reenactments and cell phone footage to depict the events of the day, during which what seemed like the entire community surrounded an immigration van and demanded the release of the detainees.</p><p>“Solidarity being worked out by people who didn't know each other — and you know, some were neighbors — but that, to me, was extraordinary,” said Sierra, the filmmaker, who screened his movie in Budapest days before Prime Minister Viktor Orbán <a class="Link" href="https://www.pbs.org/newshour/world/hungarys-orban-calls-for-his-partys-renewal-after-stunning-election-defeat" target="_blank" ><u>was defeated</u></a>. Of his time in Hungary, Sierra said, “I think the people getting to know about the protest itself, they keep saying, it’s healing and energizing.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8f0000" name="image-8f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/03645e1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0d%2Fbf%2Fb5522eb44a20ab0fc453a2da5839%2Feverybody-to-kenmure-street-still-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9fa0eea/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0d%2Fbf%2Fb5522eb44a20ab0fc453a2da5839%2Feverybody-to-kenmure-street-still-1.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2ba1d2a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0d%2Fbf%2Fb5522eb44a20ab0fc453a2da5839%2Feverybody-to-kenmure-street-still-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A still from Everybody To Kenmure Street by Felipe Bustos Sierra, an official selection of the 2026 Sundance Film Festival. Courtesy of Sundance Institute." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2ba1d2a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0d%2Fbf%2Fb5522eb44a20ab0fc453a2da5839%2Feverybody-to-kenmure-street-still-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7471d74/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0d%2Fbf%2Fb5522eb44a20ab0fc453a2da5839%2Feverybody-to-kenmure-street-still-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2ba1d2a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0d%2Fbf%2Fb5522eb44a20ab0fc453a2da5839%2Feverybody-to-kenmure-street-still-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“Everybody to Kenmure Street” unfolds on a Glasgow morning in May 2021, when immigration enforcement officers attempted to detain two community members.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Sundance Institute</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Typically, events like Doc10 would attempt to be as nonpartisan as possible. And while Cohen says the event itself doesn’t take a particular side, in the aftermath of losing government support, organizers now have more freedom to lean into opinions on fraught political topics.</p><p>“We’re obviously still nonpartisan, but that doesn't mean we can’t have a point of view,” said Cohen. “We're taking a point of view over things that shouldn't be controversial — like, we want free and fair elections. And democracy should be the winner in this next election.”</p><p>For the organizers, the strong stance is the result of feeling attacked.</p><p>“I just feel like when somebody threatens your livelihood, you gotta come back fighting bigger and stronger,” said Froehle. “There is power in numbers and people coming out. We have a great program that demonstrates how documentary filmmakers have the ability to spend more time with their subjects to get into the deeper, more intimate, emotional complexities of an experience.”</p>
entries.author: Mike Davis | WBEZ

entries.title: Gold pocket watch of Titanic’s wealthiest passenger goes up for sale in Chicago
entries.summary: <p>John Jacob Astor IV kissed his pregnant wife goodbye, reassuring her that they would see each other again soon in New York.</p><p>As Lifeboat No. 4, with Madeleine Astor in it, creaked toward the frigid North Atlantic Ocean, her husband stood above on the deck of the Titanic, smoking a cigarette with other first-class passengers.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-5e0000" name="image-5e0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>John Jacob Astor IV’s watch and gold pencil case are going on the auction block April 22 at Freeman’s auction house in the West Loop.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In a pocket of Astor’s blue serge suit was an 18-karat gold Patek Philippe watch engraved with his initials. That watch would be one of several items retrieved from Astor’s pockets as his body lay in a make-shift morgue in Halifax, Nova Scotia, days after the Titanic sank April 15, 1912.</p><p>The watch, along with Astor’s gold pencil case, are going on the auction block at 10 a.m. April 22 at Freeman’s auction house, 1550 W. Carroll Ave., in the West Loop.</p><p>The watch has attracted “global interest,” said Reginald Brack, Freeman’s watch expert.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>John Jacob Astor IV’s 18-karat yellow gold pocket watch, made by Patek Philippe for Tiffany & Co. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“We have a very healthy amount of interest. We have institutional interest. We have interest from private collectors. … It’s going to be an exciting day for sure to see where this ends up,” Brack said.</p><p>The watch, a timepiece Astor bought from Tiffany & Co.’s flagship store in New York, is expected to fetch somewhere between $300,000 and $500,000, according to Freeman’s. It was made in 1904, must be wound by hand and “keeps good time,” Brack said.</p><p>Astor was arguably the most famous person on board the doomed ship, and certainly the most wealthy — a New York real estate baron whose father, of the same name, was America’s first multi-millionaire.</p><p>Astor was an “ungainly,” lanky figure who, despite being part of New York’s most elite society, was socially awkward, said Hugh Brewster, a Canadian author who has written several books about the White Star Line’s most infamous ship, including, “Gilded Lives, Fatal Voyage: The Titanic’s First-Class Passengers and Their World.”</p><p>Astor had also written a science fiction novel, “A Journey in Other Worlds,” and was an amateur inventor, whose work included a bicycle brake.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-660000" name="image-660000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0086d4f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1440x808+0+512/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F9e%2F5fd03e1a47b68e0632c317d994a1%2Fnew-york-american-newspaper-clipping.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="New York American_Newspaper Clipping.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0086d4f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1440x808+0+512/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F9e%2F5fd03e1a47b68e0632c317d994a1%2Fnew-york-american-newspaper-clipping.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9285367/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1440x808+0+512/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F9e%2F5fd03e1a47b68e0632c317d994a1%2Fnew-york-american-newspaper-clipping.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0086d4f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1440x808+0+512/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F9e%2F5fd03e1a47b68e0632c317d994a1%2Fnew-york-american-newspaper-clipping.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The front page of the New York American from Tuesday, April 16, 1912.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Scandal stained Astor’s life a year before his death, after he divorced his first wife, Ava, and then married teenage socialite Madeleine Force, who was almost 30 years his junior.</p><p>“It was tabloid fodder because she was only 18 and he was 47,” Brewster said.</p><p>After the couple married, they headed off on a months-long European vacation, hoping for the American gossip about them to cool off.</p><p>But they decided to return to America after she became pregnant. They boarded the Titanic in Cherbourg, France, accompanied by his personal valet, her maid and a nursemaid Astor hired because he was worried about her “delicate condition,” and their Airedale terrier, “Kitty,” Brewster said.</p><p>When the ship first struck the iceberg, Astor, upon hearing the commotion, found Capt. Edward J. Smith and said he didn’t want his wife disturbed. But as the reality of the situation sank in, he accompanied his wife to the lifeboats.</p><p>“He then asks if he can accompany her on account of her delicate condition. He was refused,” Brewster said, noting that at that point in the evacuation, only women and children were allowed into lifeboats. “He threw her his gloves, and he stood with their Airedale Kitty and watched as the boat was lowered.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/things-to-do/2026/04/15/titanic-a-voyage-through-time-chicago-vr-experience" >Immersive VR experience opening soon in Chicago lets you explore the Titanic</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Madeleine survived and gave birth to a son in August 1912. Her husband’s body was among the approximately 330 plucked from the North Atlantic. He was found wearing a life jacket.</p><p>“Most people died in about 20 minutes from hypothermia,” Brewster said.</p><p>His retrieved possessions also included a diamond ring, a belt with a gold buckle and cash in a variety of currencies, Brewster said.</p><p>Vincent Astor, John Astor’s 20-year-old son from his first marriage, inherited the watch and wore it until he died in 1959, according to Brack. “Since 1959, it’s been in various Astor vaults,” he said.</p><p>It is being offered now as part of the estate of distant Astor relative, Charlene Marshall, who died last year.</p><p>The watch is not the first Titanic-related timepiece to go on the auction block. Last year, another gold pocket watch — owned by passenger Isidor Straus, a co-owner of Macy’s department store — sold for $2.3 million in England, according to a story in The New York Times. Straus and his wife, Ida, both died when the ship went down. They were last seen sitting arm in arm on the Titanic deck.</p><p><i>To learn more about the auction, go to </i><a class="Link" href="http://www.freemansauction.com" target="_blank" ><i><u>www.freemansauction.com</u></i></a><i>.</i></p>
entries.author: Stefano Esposito

entries.title: O'Neill Burke wouldn't denounce Trump because she wanted to 'maintain' relationship with feds, emails show
entries.summary: <p>Cook County’s top prosecutor declined<b> </b>to criticize the Trump administration in the weeks leading up to Operation Midway Blitz because she wanted to keep her “excellent working relationships”<b> </b>with federal law enforcement, newly filed court records show.</p><p>It’s evidence that Cook County State’s Attorney Eileen<b> </b>O’Neill<b> </b>Burke<b> </b>has a conflict that justifies the installation of a special prosecutor who could investigate the federal agents behind last fall’s deportation campaign, a coalition of elected officials and community members argued. </p><p>"The State's Attorney has an alliance with federal law enforcement that prevents her from taking action against the federal agents alleged to have committed crimes during Operation Midway Blitz," its lawyers wrote<b> </b>in a 30-page court filing Friday.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/24/chicago-cook-county-trump-ice-border-patrol-midway-blitz-special-prosecutor-oneill-burke-loevy" >O’Neill Burke rejects bid for special prosecutor to target ICE, says it threatens to ‘thwart’ justice</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>But O'Neill Burke's office insisted Monday she's<b> </b>"horrified by the thuggish and inappropriate conduct of [U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement] agents in Chicago and elsewhere." </p><p>"The petition seeking a special prosecutor is frivolous, contains baseless allegations and gross misrepresentations of the law, and will make it more difficult for the Cook County State's Attorney's Office to successfully do its job protecting the public," O'Neill Burke's office said in a statement. </p><p>The fight over whether a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-cook-county-oneill-burke-trump-ice-border-patrol-midway-blitz-special-prosecutor" target="_blank" >special prosecutor should be appointed</a> could come to a head soon, as Cook County Circuit Judge Erica<b> </b>Reddick<b> </b>is set to hear arguments Friday. She is the presiding judge<b> </b>of the court’s criminal division. O'Neill Burke's office promised a "comprehensive response"<b> </b>during the arguments.</p><p>The coalition seeking a special prosecutor pointed in its new filing to an Aug. 11 email. Sent by a member of the Justice Advisory Council, it discussed an effort by Cook County Board President Toni<b> </b>Preckwinkle’s office<b> </b>to get county agencies<b> </b>to sign on to a statement “responding to the recent actions and rhetoric coming out of the Trump administration.”</p><p>President Donald Trump<b> </b><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/08/11/donald-trump-trump-federal-takeover-dc-police-national-guard-chicago-threat-jb-pritzker" target="_blank" >threatened<b> </b>that same day</a> to deploy the National Guard into Chicago. Trump eventually tried to follow through on that threat, but he was blocked<b> </b>first by <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/10/09/federal-judge-set-to-hear-arguments-in-high-stakes-national-guard-hearing" target="_blank" >U.S. District Judge April Perry</a><b> </b>and eventually the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/12/23/supreme-court-trump-national-guard-illinois" target="_blank" >U.S. Supreme Court</a>.</p><p>Then-O'Neill Burke spokesman Matt<b> </b>McGrath<b> </b>responded<b> </b>Aug. 12, the morning after Trump’s threat.</p><p>“We obviously share concerns about Trump’s actions, rhetoric, and bluster,” he wrote. “At the same time the State’s Attorney’s top priority remains combating illegal guns, and to continue doing that effectively we need to maintain our excellent working relationships with the local (Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives) and other federal partners. So we will not be able to sign on.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fb0000" name="image-fb0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9481271/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3936x2209+0+212/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F29%2Fe67bc0c34d22ba9d56e96f32e594%2Fap25029702146868.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5107df9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3936x2209+0+212/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F29%2Fe67bc0c34d22ba9d56e96f32e594%2Fap25029702146868.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6d2e98b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3936x2209+0+212/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F29%2Fe67bc0c34d22ba9d56e96f32e594%2Fap25029702146868.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="President Donald Trump speaks before signing the Laken Riley Act in the East Room of the White House, Wednesday, Jan. 29, 2025, in Washington. (AP Photo/Alex Brandon)" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6d2e98b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3936x2209+0+212/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F29%2Fe67bc0c34d22ba9d56e96f32e594%2Fap25029702146868.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6d2e98b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3936x2209+0+212/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F29%2Fe67bc0c34d22ba9d56e96f32e594%2Fap25029702146868.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>President Donald Trump speaks before signing the Laken Riley Act in the East Room of the White House on Jan. 29, 2025.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Alex Brandon/AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Operation Midway Blitz began four weeks after the email, a copy of which was filed Friday with the coalition’s latest brief in the special prosecutor litigation. </p><p>“If the state’s attorney felt that her conflict arising from federal collaborations prevented her from even opposing the Trump Administration’s threats to Chicago, then surely the same conflict prevents her from investigating and prosecuting federal agents who carried out those threats shortly thereafter,” the coalition’s attorneys wrote in their filing Friday.</p><p>The coalition seeking a special prosecutor is made up of more than 200 elected officials, clergy, journalists and attorneys. It includes the Chicago News Guild,<b> </b>which represents journalists at the Chicago Sun-Times. The effort is led in court by the Loevy and Loevy law firm.</p><p>Prosecuting federal agents in state court would require <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/08/can-state-officials-prosecute-the-feds-calls-grow-after-minneapolis-shooting" target="_blank" >showing they acted unreasonably</a> while fulfilling their duties. Meanwhile, prosecutors like O’Neill Burke are entrusted with discretion to choose which cases to pursue.</p><p>O’Neill Burke has insisted any investigation must be conducted by law enforcement. She’s also pointed to a <a class="Link" href="https://www.illinoiscourts.gov/resources/bb31d7b0-e90c-4843-8486-c740ae2624ef/file" target="_blank" >2017 Illinois Supreme Court ruling</a> she says limits her own ability to investigate the agents.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-cook-county-oneill-burke-trump-ice-border-patrol-midway-blitz-special-prosecutor" >Seeking special prosecutor to target ICE, coalition says O’Neill Burke turned ‘blind eye’ to feds’ violence</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The high court found that a state’s attorney’s duty to investigate is “limited to circumstances where other law enforcement agencies inadequately deal with such investigation … or where a law enforcement agency asks the state’s attorney for assistance.”</p><p>But the coalition argued Friday that “it is perfectly clear that local police are failing to investigate.” They pointed to the lack of any probe by the Franklin Park or Chicago police<b> </b>into the September killing of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/09/15/silverio-villegas-gonzalez-fatally-shot-by-ice-agents-touched-the-lives-of-so-many-loved-ones-say" target="_blank" >Silverio<b> </b>Villegas<b> </b>Gonzalez</a><b> </b>by a federal agent.</p><p>The coalition argued that circumstances now are similar to those that led to special prosecutors in two previous high-profile cases: The death of Laquan<b> </b>McDonald<b> </b>at the hands of Chicago police officer Jason Van Dyke, and of David<b> </b>Koschman<b> </b>at the hands of former Mayor Richard M. Daley’s nephew<b> </b>Richard<b> </b>J. “R.J.”<b> </b>Vanecko.</p><p>“There is ample basis for the inference that the state’s attorney’s failure to act is because she is beholden to law enforcement and unwilling to risk impairing political and institutional relationships,” the coalition’s attorneys wrote.<b> </b>“Just as in those cases, a special prosecutor should be appointed here.”</p><p><i>Neither the reporters nor editors who worked on this story — including some represented by the News Guild — have been involved in the litigation described in this article.</i></p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: Review: Court Theatre’s ‘Out Here’ searches for, rather than delivers, a coherent score
entries.summary: <p>It’s best not to think of the likably quirky but not-yet-fully-realized new work “Out Here” as musical theater in the traditional sense, even though it may be impossible to avoid those expectations. After all, it possesses all the trappings of the form: a family story, characters who sing, a small orchestra, a recognizable house setting, all offered at a very traditional venue — the Court Theatre — as part of its subscription theater season.</p><p>But “Out Here” is often less like, say, “Falsettos”— a 2024 Court hit to which this bears some surface resemblances — and closer to a music/theater version of work from form-pushing experimentalists like clown/theater troupe 500 Clown and dance/theater company Lucky Plush. That makes sense, since Leslie Buxbaum, who worked extensively with both of those groups, wrote the book and co-wrote the lyrics with composer Erin McKeown.</p><p>Buxbaum’s bio even refers to a current project with Lucky Plush, a take on Luigi Pirandello’s “Six Characters in Search of an Author,” a play that seems a direct inspiration for the way “Out Here” dissolves the fourth wall from the start and searches for, rather than delivers, a coherent score.</p><div class="RatingCard Enhancement" data-module data-rating="3.0" data-align-center> <div class="RatingCard-title"> ‘Out Here’ </div> <div class="RatingCard-content"> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> </div> </div><p></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-f00000" name="module-f00000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>When:</b> Through May 10<br><b>Where:</b> Court Theater, 5535 S. Ellis Ave.<br><b>Tickets:</b> $60-$90<br><b>Info:</b> <a class="Link" href="https://www.courttheatre.org/season-tickets/2025-2026-season/out-here/" target="_blank" >courttheatre.org</a><br><b>Run time:</b> 1 hour and 40 minutes with no intermission</div> </div><p>As the show opens, a 50-ish woman named Dawn (Becca Ayers), her husband, Brian (Cliff Chamberlain), and their 16-year-old daughter, Cleo (Ellie Duffey), all hear a repetitive musical sound. Their first thought is that it’s one of their phones, but after ruling that out, they investigate the microwave, then the dishwasher. Finally, Cleo looks out the imaginary window, straight at the audience.</p><p>“Mom, Dad,” she says. “There are people here.”</p><p>After a few attempts to define exactly why they have an audience — and a surprise band on the second level of their house! — they simply accept and begin, self-consciously, to introduce themselves and their lives.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e946cc0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7818x4388+0+623/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc4%2F8f%2F181981ea4af9bec7f3547b265f52%2Fphoto-of-alex-goodrich-ellie-duffey-and-becca-ayers-by-michael-brosilow.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/943bc1e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7818x4388+0+623/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc4%2F8f%2F181981ea4af9bec7f3547b265f52%2Fphoto-of-alex-goodrich-ellie-duffey-and-becca-ayers-by-michael-brosilow.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Alex Goodrich, Ellie Duffey and Becca Ayers in “Out Here.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Michael Brosilow</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Soon, Cleo is sent out of the room for a more traditional scene — albeit one where neither character is quite sure whether they’re singing or talking — where Dawn informs Brian that she wants both of them to have joy and therefore the marriage needs to end.</p><p>Over the next 90 minutes or so, some additional narrative will follow: We’ll meet Dawn’s ex and, everyone else but Dawn seems sure, her true love Robin (Bethany Thomas) and Robin’s nonbinary young adult child Jett (Z Mowry), as well as a love interest for Brian named Gina (Amanda Pulcini), who emerges from, yes, the audience.</p><p>In terms of story, if that sounds exceedingly generic, it sure is. The creative elements here focus less on making us know or care about the characters than riffing — as a dance or clown act might — on themes such as change and the resistance to it, divorce as a complex mediation (with Alex Goodrich stepping in from the band when a mediator is called for), and life as an improvisation where you can't go back.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9c2a3b1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5557x3119+0+2350/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc8%2F79%2F01bcccb44f55a9f0a8061f4546ce%2Fphoto-of-bethany-thomas-and-becca-ayers-by-michael-brosilow.jpg width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/71bb308/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5557x3119+0+2350/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc8%2F79%2F01bcccb44f55a9f0a8061f4546ce%2Fphoto-of-bethany-thomas-and-becca-ayers-by-michael-brosilow.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Photo of Bethany Thomas and Becca Ayers by Michael Brosilow.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/71bb308/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5557x3119+0+2350/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc8%2F79%2F01bcccb44f55a9f0a8061f4546ce%2Fphoto-of-bethany-thomas-and-becca-ayers-by-michael-brosilow.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4534e95/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5557x3119+0+2350/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc8%2F79%2F01bcccb44f55a9f0a8061f4546ce%2Fphoto-of-bethany-thomas-and-becca-ayers-by-michael-brosilow.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/71bb308/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5557x3119+0+2350/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc8%2F79%2F01bcccb44f55a9f0a8061f4546ce%2Fphoto-of-bethany-thomas-and-becca-ayers-by-michael-brosilow.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Bethany Thomas (left) plays Robin, Dawn’s (right) ex, in “Out Here.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Michael Brosilow</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>McKeown’s varied music — sometimes melodic, sometimes dissonant — forms songs that are frequently, and intentionally, incomplete, as characters unsuccessfully seek to express their feelings, their intentions or even just their thoughts. Dawn ultimately becomes the clear main character — nothing seems to be stopping her joy other than herself — but nearly everyone else gets a close-to-complete song while she mostly remains in search of hers.</p><p>There’s a lot to like about “Out Here.” The actors are all skilled, the conception has an ambitious sense of artistic self-questioning and there’s some fun light comedy as it plays with its meta-theatricality. Director Chay Yew grasps and embraces the stripped-down, relaxed state of performance required to bond with an acknowledged audience and establish a constant state of becoming. There are moments when the show charms.</p><p>But there is also something lacking at the core. Even a show that desires to dissect or contemplate musicals even more than to be one, or to expose the form as a metaphor for life, has to provide enough substance to sing, or not sing, about. That might be narrative, a complex structure to puzzle together, character depth and discovery, or something else.</p><p>“Out Here” might well mean out in the sense of sexuality, and out of one’s comfort zone, and out in a world where you feel judged by people and can’t undo mistakes. But evocation of a theme based on a thin, overly ordinary tale of underdefined characters does not make for a complete artistic evening.</p>
entries.author: Steven Oxman | For the Sun-Times

entries.title: Bulls coach Billy Donovan steps away after six seasons at the helm
entries.summary: <p>Don’t hand out minutes to rookies just because.</p><p>Don’t let others make decisions that you should make for yourself.</p><p>Don’t stand in the way of progress.</p><p>This is the code of honor that Billy Donovan operates by, and the latter was the primary reason he walked away from the Bulls’ head coaching chair after six seasons.</p><p>Exactly one week after meeting with owners Michael and Jerry Reinsdorf to have a heart-to-heart about the future of the organization, the team announced on Tuesday a mutual parting of ways with Donovan.</p><p>Not that it came as much of a shock since the April 14 summit with the Reinsdorfs, as Donovan left the meeting without giving an answer on whether he wanted to stay or go. That meant more time to weigh what he really wanted, and also a chance to really step back from the situation to focus on his priorities. It became apparent to the Hall of Famer that priority No. 1 was not being a hindrance to a new front office because he stayed on.</p><p>If the Bulls were looking to operate with a clean slate, in Donovan’s mind it should be a completely clean slate.</p><p>Also playing a factor was that the Bulls still felt like a fixer-upper. Donovan stressed several times that he missed the idea of coaching meaningful NBA games in May and June rather than having exit meetings in April.</p><p>Those breadcrumbs were put down weeks ago.</p><p>“When I got into coaching I never did it for the money piece of it and I never really did it for notoriety,” Donovan said. “I love the game, I loved competing, and I felt like outside my parents, the people that had the most influence on me in my life were the guys that coached me.</p><p>“I never really thought, ‘Hey listen, I’m going to get into coaching because I want to be in the Hall of Fame.’ I wanted to get into coaching to win and the competing part, and trying to get a group of guys how to compete, so I never looked at it from a legacy standpoint as much as the season comes to an end, how are we going to work to get into a place where we’re really competing and how do we build this out moving forward? Those are the things that we need to talk about.”</p><p>Donovan and the Reinsdorfs did – for hours. And while the Bulls are in a really good spot with cap space, two first-round picks, and two solid foundation pieces in Josh Giddey and Matas Buzelis, it is still a roster with a lot of holes and possibly being at least three years away from becoming serious, and that’s if everything is done right by the new front office.</p><p>At age 60, Donovan couldn’t afford to take that trust fall.</p><p>“Selfishly, competitively, I want us to be in that situation where we’re playing in real legitimate (games),” Donovan said. “You’re in the playoffs, you’re going through a seven-game series and you’re trying to advance and move on.”</p><p>Something Donovan and his recently fired front office of executive vice president of basketball operations Arturas Karnisovas and general manager Marc Eversley couldn’t do, making the postseason just once and winning just one playoff game.</p><p>Karnisovas and Eversley lost their jobs in the wake of that failure earlier this month, but Michael Reinsdorf was still all in on Donovan, despite the 226-256 (.469 winning percentage) record. Enough so that he made it clear that the new front office would have to be good with inheriting the coach.</p><p>While Donovan appreciated the sentiment and relationship, he’s also learned in his Hall of Fame career that it’s better to feel like a sail rather than an anchor.</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

JB Pritzker plans to revamp the Illinois Gaming Board in a dramatic reorganization, potentially eliminating key aspects of public transparency that have existed for decades.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Casinos as well as video and sports gambling have exploded across the state on Pritzker's watch, and video poker could expand further if it happens in Chicago. Tasked with regulating it all is the gaming board, which reports to Pritzker and has struggled with a main task: Ensuring the integrity of the billion-dollar industry and keeping people with ties to organized crime out of it.</p><p><b>The plan</b>: In short, there would no longer be members of the gaming board, nor of the Illinois Racing Board — their functions would be merged. Deliberations and voting on gaming licenses, qualifications, discipline and other gaming matters, as well as the racing board's functions, would all be folded into a new or existing arm of state government, operating like a department of the executive branch.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikjllt-l-h/" target="_blank" ><b>READ wanted to ‘maintain’ relationship with feds, emails show</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikjllt-l-u/" target="_blank" >Jon Seidel</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikjllt-l-o/" target="_blank" >Sophie Sherry</a></h5><p><b>Criticism quelled</b>: Cook County State’s Attorney Eileen O’Neill Burke declined to criticize the Trump administration in the weeks leading up to Operation Midway Blitz because she wanted to keep her "excellent working relationships" with federal law enforcement, emails in newly filed court records show. An Aug. 11 email discussed an effort by Cook County Board President Toni Preckwinkle’s office to get county agencies to sign onto a statement "responding to the recent actions and rhetoric coming out of the Trump administration."</p><p><b>Calls for special prosecutor</b>: It’s evidence that Cook County’s top prosecutor has a conflict that justifies the installation of a special prosecutor who could investigate the feds behind last fall’s deportation campaign, argues a coalition that is made up of more than 200 elected officials, clergy, attorneys and  journalists, including the Chicago News Guild, which represents members at the Sun-Times.</p><p><b>What's next</b>: The fight over appointing a special prosecutor could come to a head as Cook County Circuit Judge Erica Reddick is set to hear arguments Friday. She is the presiding judge of the court’s criminal division. O’Neill Burke’s office promised a "comprehensive response."</p><p><b>More</b>: <br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; the lawsuit that challenged them</a>.<br> </li><li>For the second year in a row, the annual <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikjllt-l-n/" target="_blank" >Cinco de Mayo >Lawmakers sweeten pot to keep Bears in Illinois with stadium bill</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikjllt-l-c/" target="_blank" >Fran Spielman</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikjllt-l-q/" target="_blank" >Tina Sfondeles</a></h5><h5></h5><p><b>Property tax relief?</b>: Illinois hopes to take the lead over Indiana in the Chicago Bears stadium battle with a new proposal that would give the NFL team property tax certainty while also providing statewide property tax relief. State Rep. Kam Buckner, D-Chicago, plans to brief Illinois House Democrats on the new amendment Tuesday. </p><p><b>The plan</b>: The PILOT measure, shorthand for payment in lieu of taxes, would allow the Bears to renegotiate their property taxes with Arlington Heights. The property tax relief element, a new addition to stadium-related legislation in Springfield, is essential in getting support from lawmakers outside of Chicago, including Republicans.</p><p><b>What's next</b>: Buckner told the Sun-Times he would push for lawmakers to hold a committee hearing on the measure and get the proposal to the floor this week, depending on how much support, or resistance, he hears in the caucus.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikjllt-l-a/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>MORE NEWS YOU NEED 🗞️</h4><div Work began Friday on the 100-foot-long pergola at 3330 W. Arthington St. </p><p><b>Key context</b>: The structure is the centerpiece of the nearly 2-acre garden built in 1907 as a rest spot for employees at what was then Sears’ sprawling campus headquarters. The $1 million restoration is being funded by a grant from the Richard H. Driehaus Foundation — the first step in a larger effort to revive the entire garden, an idea launched by the North Lawndale Community Coordinating Council’s Greening, Open Space, Water, Soil and Sustainability committee in 2021.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikjllt-l-yh/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>MUST-READ COMMENTARY Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikjllt-l-tr/" target="_blank" >Pilsen artist’s poetry snippets are painted around Chicago and the country</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikjllt-l-ty/" target="_blank" >Genevieve Bookwalter</a></h5><h5></h5><p>You’ve probably seen her poetry by now.</p><p>Maybe it was spray-painted on the sidewalk outside a coffee shop or record store, or wheat-pasted on a utility pole that you walked by. Maybe you found the words within a giant heart painted inside an abandoned, run-down building you decided to explore.</p><p>The Pilsen artist known as Boots, whose real name is Kimberly Brown, has painted thousands of her original poetry snippets on sidewalks and elsewhere around Chicago since she began sharing her words with the world just before the pandemic.</p><p>Now, you can find her painted poetry while walking through towns in all 50 states, part of a project that Brown says has "become bigger than me at this point." All of them are tagged @poetrybyboots, which is her Instagram handle. The biggest number by far are in Chicago.</p><p>"It’s always extra special when my hometown appreciates it," says Brown, who receives tags, photos and messages nearly every day from fans who found her work. "It still blows my mind how many messages I’ll get."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikjllt-l-tj/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p>Yesterday, we asked you: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikjllt-l-ti/" target="_blank" >Should Illinois ban cellphones in classrooms during school hours?</a></p><p>Here’s some of what you said…</p><p>"I think such a law, while popular, may be unenforceable. A better solution would be to jam the signal in classrooms; that way students cannot access distractions on their phones." — <i>Patricia Boughton</i></p><p>"I am a former Illinois student ... I believe with all the school shootings that go on around the country, these students need their phones. What if something happens to the teacher and no one else has a phone to call for help in that classroom? ... One more phone could save countless lives!" — <i>Gregory Bell-Bey</i></p><p>"I do think that cellphones should be banned during school hours. They provide a distraction from education and a barrier for teachers trying to communicate ... Schools should also ensure that students will have easy access to an office phone if they need to call their parents or guardians." — <i>Margaret McCoy</i></p><p>"I'm finishing my 22nd year as a CPS high school teacher. Brain rot is real. Critical thinking has atrophied. Attention spans are measured in seconds. Students are addicted to the dopamine hits endless scrolling or game-playing ... provide. I was an early adopter of Google Classroom, which I still use ... However, I now make paper copies and direct students to put their Chromebooks and phones away. It's a daily battle. I can't tell you how many times a student has told me, 'I'm talking to my mom." — <i>Sharon Marzano</i></p><p>"Absolutely! As an educator for more than 40 years, I can say ... It distracts students from focusing ... even if the phone is in a backpack and on silent, the temptation to check it is overwhelming. It fractures concentration, and over time leads to decreased attention span. It also contributes to diminished social skills, as kids are more focused on their devices than on human interaction. When phones are not allowed in school, teachers report students are way more engaged with learning and with one another." — <i>Anita Caref</i></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikjllt-l-td/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i> </p>
entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: Everson Pereira breathing some life into White Sox outfield
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — It’s starting to click for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> outfielder Everson Pereira.</p><p>The 25-year-old has posted a 1.015 OPS entering their series opener at Arizona. He’s playing a reliable right field — no small feat for a team that had fielded a rotating cast in recent years — and he’s injecting some excitement to the Sox roster with his power and his penchant for showing some passion on the field.</p><p>The encouraging early returns have come after fits and starts to Pereira’s young career — and all it took was a little breather.</p><p>“Basically, the main thing was for me to be calm and to breathe, and find a stance that I feel comfortable with,” Pereira said through an interpreter before another multi-hit day at the plate over the weekend against the Athletics.</p><p>He's smacked three homers and four doubles in his first 10 games with the Sox, a promising introduction (11-for-37, 12 strikeouts, three walks) that was interrupted by a stint on the injured list with a sprained ankle. But his arrival so far has been a breath of fresh air for the Sox, who need more pop in the batting order beyond <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/19/noah-schultz-munetaka-murakami-white-sox-athletics" target="_blank" ><u>Munetaka Murakami</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/18/colson-montgomery-white-sox-homers" target="_blank" ><u>Colson Montgomery</u></a>.</p><p>Pereira credits Sox coaches with helping him find the swing he couldn’t consistently produce during short-lived big-league stints with the Yankees in 2023 (.151/.233/.194 in 93 at-bats) and the Rays last year (.138/.219/.246 in 65 at-bats).</p><p>If he can rein in the strikeout rate and keep slugging, it’ll make the Sox’ offseason trade with Tampa Bay look <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/11/20/everson-pereira-hoping-to-take-advantage-of-opportunity-with-white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>all the more fruitful</u></a>. General manager Chris Getz acquired Pereira along with utility man Tanner Murray in exchange for swing man Yoendrys Gómez and reliever Steven Wilson.</p><p>“He's a really toolsy guy. He can do a lot of things out there,” manager Will Venable said. “Obviously, the power is one of the things that leads his skillset. You know if he touches it, it goes. So I really like what he does out there.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="pt" dir="ltr">Pereira sent someone a souvenir! <a href="https://t.co/HfxWFz5YMK">pic.twitter.com/HfxWFz5YMK</a></p>— Chicago White Sox (@whitesox) <a href="https://twitter.com/whitesox/status/2044881595107078407?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 16, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Big swings are nothing new for the Venezuelan outfielder, who clubbed 83 homers across five minor-league seasons.</p><p>“My goal has always been to hit the ball hard, ever since I was a kid. That’s what I focused on,” Pereira said. “I’m pretty good with my swing right now. They found a position for me to feel more comfortable with my stance, and what I did was put my hands a little further or higher, and from there I feel more comfortable. It’s an easier path [for] my swing to the ball.”</p><p>While it didn't pan out for Pereira as a top-100 prospect ascending New York’s system, the opportunity is there for the taking on a rebuilding squad like it is for plenty other fliers the Sox have taken on their roster.</p><p>“We want these guys to be comfortable and be themselves,” Venable said. “Some of these guys are guys who have had opportunities in other places that maybe didn't work out, and they're going to have some runway here. So we just want to give them that confidence and that comfortability to go out and be themselves and perform with their best.”</p><p>“He's a good defender, good arm, and does some nice things on the bases. So he's been a big part of this,” Venable said of Pereira, who’s trying to keep things simple and appreciate the ride.</p><p>“I’m just enjoying this moment. I’m enjoying being in the major leagues,” Pereira said. “Before, for whatever reason, I wasn’t able to do it, and before the season I said, ‘You know what, I have to enjoy it.’ That’s why you can see all the emotions that come out when I do something.”</p><p>And he thinks that attitude is catching on in the clubhouse.</p><p>“I’m going to spread my love for the game with everybody. And even if I have a bad game, I just want to support my teammates and do everything I can to keep the hype,” he said. “I’ve [been] able to do it this year. That was a switch I was able to turn on.”</p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a
