entries.title: El 1 de mayo habrá clases en CPS, pero acuerdo con sindicato anima a estudiantes a acudir a la marcha
entries.summary: <p>Las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago (CPS) estarán en sesión durante un día completo el 1 de mayo, pero también será un día cívico oficial de acción durante el cual cientos de estudiantes podrán participar en una "excursión" a una masiva manifestación pro-trabajo y anti-presidente Donald Trump.</p><p>El Sindicato de Maestros de Chicago (CTU) <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/03/12/chicago-teachers-union-plans-to-join-may-1-no-school-no-work-day-of-action-wants-classes-canceled" target="_blank" >quería que el distrito escolar cancelara las clases</a> para que el personal y los estudiantes pudieran participar en el Día Internacional de los Trabajadores, también conocido como May Day en inglés.</p><p>Sin embargo, la directora ejecutiva del CPS, Macquline King, se opuso, diciendo que quería mantener las escuelas en funcionamiento. El desacuerdo desató una <a class="Link" href="https://www.chalkbeat.org/chicago/2026/04/09/cps-will-be-open-may-1-during-protest-ceo-says-but-board-could-overrule-her/" target="_blank" >tormenta de debate</a>.</p><p>El jueves, las partes acordaron un compromiso.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-af0000" name="module-af0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Según un <a class="Link" href="https://docs.google.com/document/d/10MIwY2T4ikmIpo6q1siwVvxbTyjp72qdPbXalwqy0c8/edit?usp=sharing" target="_blank" >acuerdo entre CPS y el CTU</a>, CPS observará este 1 de mayo de 2026 como un día de compromiso cívico en todo el distrito.</p><p>Con ese fin, se proporcionarán autobuses y almuerzos en bolsa a 100 escuelas para que los estudiantes puedan asistir a una manifestación del 1 de mayo por la tarde. Si el distrito no puede encontrar suficientes autobuses, la Municipalidad pagará tarjetas de autobús.</p><p>El distrito también enviará orientación a los líderes escolares sobre la importancia del compromiso cívico y sugerirá formas de involucrar a los estudiantes.</p><p>El acuerdo establece que el CPS no puede "tomar represalias contra" los miembros del personal que se tomen el día libre para participar en actividades cívicas. CPS señala que la ley estatal permite que los estudiantes estén ausentes un día por un "evento cívico", pero deben contar con el permiso de sus padres para hacerlo.</p><p>El acuerdo también libera a 65 maestros para ir a Springfield a hacer cabildeo por más fondos estatales el 13 y el 27 de mayo. Los líderes anteriores de CPS han resistido liberar al personal para este tipo de abogacía, a pesar de que el distrito escolar necesita urgentemente fondos adicionales, en gran parte debido a la forma en que el estado financia las escuelas.</p><p>Y el memorándum designa el 1 de mayo, 2028, la próxima vez que el 1 de mayo sea en un día escolar, como un día profesional dirigido por los maestros, el cual los maestros podrían utilizar para la acción cívica. El acuerdo también crea un "grupo de trabajo del 1 de mayo" entre el distrito y CTU para "implementar actividades curriculares y basadas en la escuela".</p><p>Bridget Doherty Trebing, maestra en la Secundaria Taft ubicada en el lado noroeste, dijo que piensa que este compromiso logra el objetivo del CTU. Llamó al acuerdo "innovador".</p><p>"Hemos estado pidiendo todo el tiempo ser socios del distrito y parece que eso es lo que ha sucedido", comentó. "Mis estudiantes han estado preguntando directamente ¿cuándo más adultos van a tomar una postura? Están prestando atención a lo que está sucediendo en el mundo. Así que este es un día que creo que los estudiantes quieren ver".</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.cps.edu/press-releases/2026/april/CPS-preserves-may-1-as-full-instructional-day/" target="_blank" >En una declaración emitida el viernes</a>, King también dijo que CPS logró su objetivo.</p><p>"Este acuerdo preserva el tiempo de clase que los estudiantes merecen y respeta a nuestro personal y las familias que deben poder confiar en que CPS mantendrá el calendario escolar académico", afirmó King. "Al mismo tiempo, el acuerdo honra la orgullosa historia de la acción cívica en Chicago y más allá".</p><p>Los funcionarios de CPS enfatizan que la participación en los eventos del 1 de mayo es "totalmente voluntaria y a discreción de los líderes escolares".</p><p>"Ningún estudiante está obligado a participar; puede optar por quedarse en la escuela. Ningún miembro del personal está obligado a participar, ni se excusa al personal de su deber normal para participar", según la declaración del distrito.</p><p>CTU ha estado planeando una protesta de "sin escuela, sin trabajo, sin compras" para el 1 de mayo desde hace más de un año.</p><p>Ha sido un día para mostrar apoyo a los sindicatos laborales y levantar preocupaciones sobre las acciones del gobierno, pero este año ha adquirido un nuevo significado mientras los activistas piden boicotear el trabajo para protestar contra la campaña de deportación masiva del presidente Donald Trump y otras políticas implementadas por su administración.</p><p>Pero durante semanas, no estaba claro quién estaba tomando las decisiones sobre el 1 de mayo.</p><p>Tan recientemente como la mañana del jueves, el alcalde Brandon Johnson dijo durante una aparición en WBEZ que indicó a su jefe de gabinete y a otras agencias de la Municipalidad que se encargaran de la logística del día. CTU atribuyó a Johnson el mérito por ayudar a llegar a este compromiso.</p><p>"Si vamos a transformar nuestras economías, si vamos a exigir que los ultrarricos paguen su parte justa en impuestos, las voces de los trabajadores, de la gente común, tienen que levantarse", le dijo a WBEZ.</p><p>El viernes, emitió una declaración, diciendo que estaba "contento de que todas las partes estén trabajando juntas para asegurar que las comunidades escolares puedan participar".</p><p>CTU pensaba que King se alinearía con su plan después de que la junta escolar realizara una votación informal en una sesión cerrada la semana pasada que mostró que la mayoría de los miembros apoyaban la cancelación de la escuela.</p><p>Pero incluso después de esa votación, King se opuso, diciendo que quería un día de clases para los estudiantes.</p><p>One Future Illinois, un super PAC que planea involucrarse en las próximas elecciones de la junta escolar, <a class="Link" href="https://www.politico.com/f/?id=0000019d-83a1-de9d-a9fd-97f7e2320000" target="_blank" >incluso encargó una encuesta</a> preguntando a los votantes si creían que CPS debería estar en sesión. Justo más de la mitad se opuso a la idea de cancelar la escuela, mientras que el 40% dijo que estaba de acuerdo con que se cancelara la escuela.</p><p>El gobernador JB Pritzker también echó un balde de agua fría sobre la idea de cancelar las clases para el 1 de mayo cuando se le preguntó al respecto el jueves.</p><p>"Creo que debemos asegurarnos de que nuestros niños estén recibiendo cada día de educación que merecen y que los padres exigen", dijo Pritzker. "Que los operadores políticos traten de determinar cuál debería ser el calendario para los niños parece inapropiado".</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Whitney Young High School principal shot at while driving in Washington Heights
entries.summary: <p>The principal of Whitney Young High School, Rickey Harris, escaped injury after being shot at Friday night while driving in Washington Heights on the South Side. </p><p>Around 10:15 p.m. Friday, the 53-year-old was southbound in the 1300 block of West 95th Street when gunfire struck his vehicle, Chicago police said. </p><p>"While driving, I'm shaking, realizing these were gunshots, multiple gunshots," Harris told <a class="Link" href="https://abc7chicago.com/post/washington-park-chicago-shooting-suspect-fires-whitney-young-high-school-principal-rickey-harris-throop-95th-street/18928372/" target="_blank" >ABC</a>. "If anyone's looking for a sign to believe in God, here I am. Here I am."</p><p>Harris wasn't present at the high school Tuesday, school officials said. </p><p>Area detectives were investigating the incident. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: City/Suburban Hoops Report: IHSA looks at new seeding process; Deerfield and Oswego East look for new coaches
entries.summary: <p>There has been a lot of discussion of where the IHSA seeding process is headed for basketball in this state.</p><p>How sectional seeds are determined could be changing dramatically as the IHSA is considering using the MaxPreps RPI seeding system.</p><p>The MaxPreps RPI system will be used this spring for the boys volleyball season, so the IHSA — and volleyball coaches — will get a feel for it and be able to produce some feedback.</p><p>Currently, basketball coaches hash out the seeds, submitting votes online towards the end of the season. The IHSA gathers the results from the coaches together and the brackets are released with all the sectional seeds and matchups.</p><p>The MaxPreps RPI system, which uses analytics in its algorithm, would eliminate coaches having any say in sectional seeds.</p><p>On the surface, it sounds like a sign of the times and how seeding has progressed. But is the system really that broken that it needs to be fixed?</p><p>Sure, there are some outliers. But in general there are so few questionable, eyebrow-raising seeds that are churned out from year to year.</p><p>There is no better proof of this than the fact, historically, the top seeds advance and do so consistently, particularly in the two largest classes.</p><p>If you use the past five seasons as a snapshot, there have been a combined 160 teams in 3A and 4A to advance to a sectional championship game. Only five of those 160 teams have been lower than a four seed. And 124 of those 160 title game seeds have been either a No. 1 or No. 2 seed.</p><p><b>Coaching openings</b> </p><p>There is always turnover in high school basketball coaching. A number of coaches step down following every season throughout the Chicago area.</p><p>Deerfield and Oswego East are two of the openings this spring.</p><p>While maybe not the highest profile coaching jobs, they are positions where the coaches took each program to new heights. There were a lot of wins and success under Dan McKendrick at Deerfield and Ryan Velasquez at Oswego East.</p><p>The late great Steve Pappas had smashing success at Deerfield over a three-year period in the 1990s, including back-to-back sectional championships in 1996 and 1997.</p><p>But McKendrick steps away as the winningest coach in program history, highlighted by this past year’s state runner-up finish in Class 3A.</p><p>In 14 years, McKendrick’s team won 20-plus games seven times while winning five regional championships. He finishes his career with 261 wins.</p><p>Oswego East hasn’t been around all that long with its first varsity basketball season coming in 2005-06. Velasquez has been leading the program for the past nine years, compiling an impressive overall record of 198-71.</p><p>During his time leading the program, Oswego East never won fewer than 20 games in a season, minus the Covid-shortened season when the Wolves finished 10-3.</p><p>While winning an average of 22 games a year, Velasquez led the program to its only sectional championship in 2023, a school record 33 wins in 2021-22 and six conference championships.</p><p><b>No burger boys</b></p><p>The McDonald’s All-American game was recently played, and for the third consecutive year no Illinois high school basketball player was selected.</p><p>Former Joliet West star and current Michigan State point guard Jeremy Fears Jr. was the last player selected from Illinois in 2023.</p><p>Illinois had a historic run of McDonald’s All-Americans with at least one player chosen every year from 1978-2008. But in the 11 years since 2015, Illinois has had a grand total of three McDonald’s All-Americans: Fears, Max Christie (2021) of Rolling Meadows and DJ Steward (2022) of Young.</p>
entries.author: Joe Henricksen

entries.title: Man found fatally shot in car in South Chicago
entries.summary: <p>A man was found fatally shot Tuesday morning inside a car in the South Chicago neighborhood.</p><p>The man, whose age was unknown, was found about 8:30 a.m. He was unresponsive and had been shot multiple times. The car was in the 8600 block of South DuSable Lakeshore Drive, Chicago police said.</p><p>He was pronounced dead at the scene, and remains unidentified, police said.</p><p>Area 2 detectives are investigating.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Bears GM Ryan Poles needs to draft for his defense the way he once did for offense
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> quarterback Caleb Williams spent part of Monday looking ahead.</p><p>Three of last year’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/halas-intrigue/2026/04/21/bears-nfl-draft-preview-podcast-halas-intrigue" target="_blank" >draft</a> picks became some of his most trusted weapons on offense as rookies. Their connection with the quarterback only figures to grow as they spent the next few seasons together. The same goes for receiver Rome Odunze, who was drafted the same year as Williams.</p><p>“It’s going to be really fun to go out there and grow together,” Williams said.</p><p>Hopefully, Ryan Poles took notice of Williams’ words. Because the general manager needs to do for his defense this week what the Bears did for their offense during last year’s draft. That means taking a premium player in Round 1, yes, but addressing almost every defensive position over the course of the three-day draft, which starts Thursday.</p><p>That’s what happened on offense last year, when, after taking tight end Colston Loveland in Round 1, the Bears landed standouts at wide receiver (Luther Burden) and running back (Kyle Monangai) plus help at both tackle (Ozzy Trapilo) and guard (Luke Newman). The only position group the Bears didn’t touch was quarterback.</p><p>Poles, as usual, was vague in explaining the team’s direction Tuesday, saying he had confidence the Bears front office was ready for any scenario that pops up with the first 24 picks Thursday.</p><p>“If things start to shift and move, we're agile enough to make adjustments,” hes aid. If that's moving up, moving back, we'll be ready for anything that comes our way.”</p><p>Staying exactly where they are Thursday and drafting an edge rusher 25<sup>th</sup> should be the first piece of the puzzle. The Bears have, to this point, done nothing to fix their biggest defensive problem. They finished second-to-last in pass-rush win rate last year, ahead of only the woeful Bengals. They boast only one player, Montez Sweat, who ranked in Pro Football Focus’ top 60 pass-rushing edge players — and he’s No. 39.</p><p>Auburn’s Keldric Faulk, who is only 20, is among the edge rushers who could be available at No. 25. ESPN’s projection system predicts Faulk to post 23 ½ sacks in his first five seasons in the NFL. The Bears would surely take that — in the last 30 years, the team has drafted one player who went on to more sacks through their first five years: Alex Brown. Other edge rushers who could be available when they pick include Clemson’s T.J. Parker, Missouri’s Zion Young, Miami’s Akheem Mesidor and Texas A&M’s Cashius Howell. All are flawed — Parker had only five sacks last year, Young was part of the Michigan brawl while at Michigan State, Mesidor is 25 and Howell has short arms —but that’s why they may be available 25 picks into <br>the draft.</p><p>Taking an edge rusher first would free the Bears up to fill three of their four position groups on Day 2 of the draft. Poles has two second-round picks and one third-rounder with which to maneuver.</p><p>The Bears need another defensive tackle to challenge Gervon Dexter and Grady Jarrett. Clemson’s Peter Woods, Florida’s Caleb Banks, Texas Tech’s Lee Hunter and Iowa State’s Domonique Orange could be available in Round 2.</p><p>The safety position opposite Coby Bryant is the Bears' lone open starting job. Rather than consider Toledo’s Emmanuel McNeil-Warren  or Oregon’s Dillon Theineman in Round 1, they should be able to find one in Round 3 or later. Since 2010, there have been 13 instances of a player drafted in Round 3 or later being named an all-pro safety.</p><p>The Bears could take a cornerback to challenge the mercurial Tyrique Stevenson or a linebacker to stash in case they walk away from T.J. Edwards next offseason for a paltry $2.3 million dead cap hit.</p><p>The best part of Poles’ draft last year was how he was able to stack impact offensive players at different position from round to round. He’ll have to do the same this year on defense, no matter which order he chooses.</p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Mayor Johnson, CTA president defend security firings under fire from City Council's Black Caucus
entries.summary: <p>Mayor Brandon Johnson and Acting CTA President Nora Leerhsen on Tuesday defended <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/04/20/cta-private-security-guard-contract" >their decision to replace</a> hundreds of unarmed security guards with “trained professionals,” a move drawing fire from the chair of the City Council’s Black Caucus.</p><p>Ald. Stephanie Coleman (16th) denounced the “sudden termination” of contracts with Monterrey Security and its two Black subcontractors as a “betrayal of the city’s commitment to equity” because it put more than 70 African-American security workers out of work with less than a week’s notice.</p><p>City <a class="Link" href="https://webapps1.chicago.gov/vcsearch/cta/contracts/B21OP04714B" target="_blank" >records</a> show the CTA embarked on a $44 million, three-year contract with Monterrey Security in 2022, with two one-year options to renew.</p><p></p><p>In a statement issued on behalf of the 20-member Black Caucus, Coleman demanded that the CTA suspend the Friday deadline to cancel the security contracts and convene an “urgent meeting” with the Black Caucus and the two Black sub-contractors — Kates Detective & Security and Rush Solutions — to “develop a transition strategy that includes job placement opportunity or contract extensions.”</p><p>If the CTA has concerns about security, Coleman said, “three Chicago based-firms that service socially economically-disadvantaged areas that renewed their contracts three weeks ago" should have been given an opportunity to provide “a remedy so they can further their business instead of just putting them out on the streets.”</p><p>“The notice that they were sent was, `We’re out of money.’ But the announcement to the general public is, `We’re moving in another direction with armed officers.’ If the direction of the authority was to go toward armed security guards, both of these firms are former sheriffs," Coleman told the Sun-Times.</p><p>Coleman blamed a “lack of leadership” for the sudden nature of the firings. She noted that Leerhsen has served as acting CTA president for nearly 18 months because Johnson has yet to name a permanent replacement for Dorval Carter Jr.</p><p>"I blame a system that has failed Black and Brown people and I blame a system that is failing Chicago-based firms that employ those in socially economic disadvantaged areas, and my job is to speak up for... those 70 to 80 officers who are now without work due to an abrupt decision," she said.</p><p>At a news conference Tuesday to kick off the CTA’s construction season, Johnson was asked about the decision to cancel the contract. The mayor punted to Leerhsen, even though the question was asked of him.</p><p>“That contract — in long-established, mutually agreed upon terms — would expire when all the funds were used. And that’s what occurred. That was a long-term understanding between the parties,” Leerhsen told reporters at the 51st Street Green Line station.</p><p>“It’s critical that we have the right resources on CTA to meet the needs of our riders. And as we grow our resources, we also have to have to be strategic and smart about what is the right resource on CTA. And that was the decision here to not continue to add funds to that contract, but move forward with other resources.”</p><p> Johnson added that “safety is our top priority… We’re gonna continue to make sure that we are moving with expediency to create a safe, accessible, reliable, affordable transit system and we’re gonna do that in an equitable way,”</p><p></p><p>Without offering specifics, Leerhsen said the law enforcement partnerships will include “new missions” across the bus and rail systems “designed to ensure our employees feel safe and our riders see a visible presence of resources.”</p><p>Leerhsen said the now-fired unarmed security guards would be replaced by sworn officers, crisis intervention workers and social service employees trained to assist riders who are homeless or have mental health issues. </p><p>“You’re gonna see uniformed officers across the system in a targeted, data-informed way that’s driven by feedback from our riders, from our employees, as well as the data that we’re seeing,” she said. “You’re also gonna see our canine guards on the system, also deployed in a strategic way, as well as upcoming new pilots of violence intervention and crisis specialists on the system. So we’re bringing a full force of a holistic approach to address the needs on our system… in addition to our longstanding partnerships with social service agencies.”</p><p>Coleman didn't buy Leerhsen's claim that the security changes were an upgrade for CTA riders.</p><p>"I don't know how you can [present] something as an improvement and it's been less than 72 hours," Coleman said.</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Review: ‘Windfall’ heartbreakingly asks if money buys justice
entries.summary: <p>For all of its unblinking, real-world brutality, “Windfall” unspools with an ethereal, dreamlike quality, nightmares included. The drama arrives at Steppenwolf as a commission from Oscar-winner <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2026/04/10/windfall-steppenwolf-play-tarell-alvin-mccraney-moonlight-writer" target="_blank" ><u>Tarell Alvin McCraney</u></a><u>.</u></p><p>As he did in the Tony-nominated drama “Choir Boy” and the Oscar-winning screenplay “Moonlight” (based on his unpublished play “In Moonlight Black Boys Look Blue”), “Windfall” lives at the intersection of crushing tragedy and indomitable humanity. Terrible things befall the characters of “Windfall,” but it doesn’t extinguish their music, their fight or their love for each other.</p><p>At the core of “Windfall” is a blunt question: How much money is a murdered Black person worth? And if that money comes from the very system capable of murder, is taking it morally reprehensible or a modicum of justice?</p><p>Director Awoye Timpo creates a world that ripples between the real and the slightly surreal. McCraney embeds all-too familiar violence and economic struggle with a glimmering sheen of the spectral. Or maybe that light is just the flare from a gunshot.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a80cb69/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1800x1010+0+221/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F73%2F9f%2F0e32c8fd48bca5dffcb9abf75c7f%2Fwindfall-2-photo-by-michael-brosilow.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9cc2afe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1800x1010+0+221/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F73%2F9f%2F0e32c8fd48bca5dffcb9abf75c7f%2Fwindfall-2-photo-by-michael-brosilow.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Esco Jouléy appears with ensemble members Namir Smallwood and Jon Michael Hill in “Windfall.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Michael Brosilow </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In "Windfall," tyrannical viciousness screams to the surface. But so does a scrim of fantastical protections: ancestral, spiritual and familial. Tinges of the supernatural aside, "Windfall" is deeply rooted in a story that’s been repeated more times than anyone will ever be able to count: This time around, it’s singing while Black that’ll get you shot.</p><p>The six-strong ensemble weaves a powerful story. At the onset, Eli (Esco Jouléy, alternately tough and vulnerable), friend Cori (<a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2024/10/25/jon-michael-hill-tv-actor-steppenwolf-lucy-and-leroy" target="_blank" ><u>Jon Michael Hill,</u></a> purveyor of much of the show’s humor) and “Brother 1” (Namir Smallwood, a magnetic presence throughout) are camped in front of the kind of black ops site overseen by Chicago cop Jon Burge, who<a class="Link" href="https://www.justice.gov/archives/opa/pr/former-chicago-police-officer-jon-burge-sentenced-lying-about-police-torture" target="_blank" ><u> tortured and abused</u></a> more than 100 people over a two-decade career, leading to false confessions and wrongful imprisonment. They’re protesting the very existence of the site, and trying to save a child who has vanished into the building.</p><p>Through song and chant, Eli, Brother and Cori heighten awareness and start a movement that snowballs into an avalanche. The cops mow down the encampment. Brother and Cori make it out. Eli gets shot. (None of this is a spoiler as it all unfolds early on.)</p><div class="RatingCard Enhancement" data-module data-rating="3.5" data-align-center> <div class="RatingCard-title"> Windfall </div> <div class="RatingCard-content"> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> </div> </div><p></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-c70000" name="module-c70000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>When:</b> Through May 31<br><b>Where:</b> Steppenwolf Ensemble Theatre, 1650 N. Halsted<br><b>Info:</b> Tickets are $20 - $148.50; run time is two hours, 10 minutes including one intermission. More info at <a class="Link" href="http://steppenwolf.org" target="_blank" ><u>Steppenwolf.org</u></a></div> </div><p>The city offers Eli's adopted father Henri “Mr. Mano” Tamaño (Michael Potts) a settlement substantial enough to let him keep his house. When Mr. Mano refuses, the plot gestures toward Charles Dickens, but inverted: Mr. Mano has four visitors – some of the flesh, some possibly not — who try to convince him to take the cash.</p><p>There’s a constant presence surrounding Mr. Mano — that of another child, Marcus (played by Glenn Davis), who has died and returned as an apparition. When an anguished Mr. Mano screams that he cannot take “blood money,” Marcus’ retort is tragically accurate: All money is blood money.</p><p>Davis captures the complex, contradictory relationship between Marcus and Mr. Mano. Marcus loves his father deeply, and is prone to screaming at him in frustration. Mr. Mano is both sympathetic and stubborn. Appeals to logic, emotion and finally threats will not change his mind. Potts’ Mr. Mano makes the money seem like Judas’ 30 pieces of silver: a betrayal of the absolute worst kind.</p><p>It may sound like “Windfall” is an exercise in grimness, but it is definitely not. Music (great work by music director Mahmoud Khan) wafts through the story from gossamer lilt to percussive stomp as Eli, Cori and Brother send their voices up to the stars, sometimes emphasizing the percussive insistence with drums and bells.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-af0000" name="image-af0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ab8d86f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1800x1010+0+199/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fec%2Fe0%2F2072e2b34135875c0e1c4860a6b1%2Fwindfall-9-photo-by-michael-brosilow.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d476afa/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1800x1010+0+199/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fec%2Fe0%2F2072e2b34135875c0e1c4860a6b1%2Fwindfall-9-photo-by-michael-brosilow.jpg data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/851a4e7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1800x1010+0+199/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fec%2Fe0%2F2072e2b34135875c0e1c4860a6b1%2Fwindfall-9-photo-by-michael-brosilow.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt=" Ensemble member Alana Arenas plays several characters who visit Michael Potts' character in "Windfall."" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/851a4e7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1800x1010+0+199/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fec%2Fe0%2F2072e2b34135875c0e1c4860a6b1%2Fwindfall-9-photo-by-michael-brosilow.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0f73bdc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1800x1010+0+199/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fec%2Fe0%2F2072e2b34135875c0e1c4860a6b1%2Fwindfall-9-photo-by-michael-brosilow.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/851a4e7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1800x1010+0+199/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fec%2Fe0%2F2072e2b34135875c0e1c4860a6b1%2Fwindfall-9-photo-by-michael-brosilow.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p> Ensemble member Alana Arenas plays several characters who visit Michael Potts’ character in “Windfall.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Michael Brosilow</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Alana Arenas is invaluable in a trio of unnerving roles, none of whom provide names: First Lady shows up to tell Mr. Mano about the settlement the city is offering for the death of his son. Miss Second is an appraiser (or an apparition — could be either) who informs Mr. Mano his taxes are in arrears and his home is about to be put up for auction. The Last One appears clad all in black like the Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come, and with a similarly dire message.</p><p>McCraney wisely doesn’t attempt to sew things together neatly — or at all really — for Mr. Mano. Part of his gift as a playwright is his ability to leave things realistically ambiguous without the play feeling unfinished. “Windfall” provides no answers, but it finds the sustaining power of music and family, with or without life-changing (blood) money.</p><p>There’s power to spare in “Windfall,” but there’s also a looseness to some scenes — the intensity incrementally flags at times, especially during moments of brief audience interaction. And while the music is immersive, it’s not always seamlessly woven into the dialogue. These are minor points — tweaks, not dealbreakers.</p><p>Finally, some context on the settlement: The amount offered to Mr. Mano for the murder of his son is less than 1% of CPD’s 2026 budget. In real life, and after costing the city some $85 million in settlements, Burge retired to Florida with<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2014/7/3/18567894/illinois-supreme-court-ruling-means-burge-can-keep-his-pension" target="_blank" ><u> his full pension. </u></a></p><p><i>Corrected: This story was updated to correct plot points regarding characters Eli and Marcus.</i></p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2026/04/10/windfall-steppenwolf-play-tarell-alvin-mccraney-moonlight-writer" >In ‘Windfall,’ Oscar winner Tarell Alvin McCraney asks if money really buys justice for Black men</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2024/10/31/jon-michael-hill-tv-actor-steppenwolf-lucy-and-leroy" >This is the year of actor Jon Michael Hill, from soulful monologues to playing bluesman Robert Johnson</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2024/03/17/phylicia-rashad-directing-black-family-drama-purpose-at-steppenwolf" >At Steppenwolf Theatre, Phylicia Rashad is reimagining the Black family drama</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Catey Sullivan | For the Sun-Times

entries.title: Cancelan desfile del Cinco de Mayo en La Villita por segundo año: ‘No hay nada que celebrar’
entries.summary: <p>Por segundo año consecutivo, el desfile anual del Cinco de Mayo en el vecindario de La Villita ha sido cancelado debido a preocupaciones sobre la aplicación de la ley de la inmigración, dijeron los organizadores.</p><p>El desfile suele atraer a cientos de personas al vecindario y celebra la victoria de México sobre el ejército francés de Napoleón III en la Batalla de Puebla en 1862. Pero este año, la comunidad mexicana de Chicago sigue en tensión tras el "Operativo Midway Blitz", la agresiva campaña de deportación que la administración de Trump lanzó en el área de Chicago el otoño pasado.</p><p>"Muchas familias están experimentando miedo e incertidumbre debido a un aumento en las acciones de aplicación de la ley de inmigración y la amenaza continua de redadas", dijeron los organizadores de la Cámara de Comercio de Cermak Road y Casa Puebla en un comunicado.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-280000" name="module-280000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Las personas todavía están evitando reuniones públicas por miedo, añadieron los organizadores. "No hay nada que celebrar".</p><p>El evento también fue <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/04/11/desfile-del-cinco-de-mayo-en-la-villita-se-cancela-por-temor-a-redadas-migratorias-la-gente-tiene-miedo" target="_blank" >cancelado el año pasado</a>, por razones similares.</p><p>"Fue una decisión difícil de tomar, pero la seguridad de nuestra comunidad es lo más importante", dijo el año pasado el organizador Héctor Escobar al Sun-Times.</p><p>Desde entonces, Chicago y los suburbios fueron objetivo de agentes federales enmascarados, quienes detuvieron a residentes mientras utilizaban irritantes químicos como gas lacrimógeno. Se llevaron a cabo protestas masivas y esfuerzos comunitarios para proteger a los inmigrantes.</p><p>Las acciones de los federales finalmente disminuyeron, pero el impacto en comunidades latinas como La Villita, Pilsen y el lado sureste ha persistido.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Mary Norkol

entries.title: What's not to love about Sky rookie Gabriela Jaquez?
entries.summary: <p>Driving from Columbus to Chicago, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> guard Jacy Sheldon couldn't wait to get in the gym.</p><p>She was dying to meet every one of her new teammates. But even amidst legends of the game like Skylar Diggins and Courtney Vandersloot, one player stood out.</p><p>Gabriela Jaquez, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/13/sky-draft-gabriela-jaquez-with-no-5-pick" >Sky’s first-round pick in the 2026 draft.</a></p><p>"She's a killer, man," Sheldon told the Sun-Times over the phone. "She wants to win. She competes on both sides of the ball. That's someone I want on my team."</p><p>Spend a day in the Sky's training camp and you'll hear one thing over and over again: everyone loves Jaquez, the No. 5 overall pick in this year's draft.</p><p>They're watching her do exactly what she did in college: knock down threes, crash the boards, and own her defensive assignments.</p><p>They're starting to see player archetypes she might fit into — veteran guard Rachel Banham told the Sun-Times Jaquez reminds her of Bridget Carleton.</p><p>In the end, Jaquez is just following the advice she's received from coaches, family, and even retired WNBA champion Sue Bird: be yourself.</p><p>A simple directive for Jaquez, who's been waiting on this moment for a long time.</p><p>"Once I realized that I wanted to play basketball in college then I was like, yeah I want to be a professional basketball player," Jaquez told the Sun-Times on Day 2 of camp. "Because of my competitive spirit. You wanna be the best you can be, and the WNBA is the best."</p><p>Jaquez is well aware of the learning curve for rookies in the W: getting used to the physicality and new terminology.</p><p>But in some ways this new chapter is easier than before.</p><p>Jaquez noted something she's heard from everyone in the pro game: that college practices are actually the hardest ones you'll ever experience.</p><p>"You have to understand in college, everyone's way younger," Jaquez said. "You have to take care of everyone's bodies differently now that, you know, people are 30, 35."</p><p>Indeed: the Sky will have five players in the 30 or older club on this year's roster. And coach Tyler Marsh is smart to be managing their load already. Some players are nursing offseason injuries, others are racing back from overseas tournaments.</p><p>Jaquez, meanwhile, is starting the season healthy, and has had a whole two weeks to rest since her last game. (In women's basketball time, that's like a whole year.)</p><p>She'll be expected to help glue their offense together, especially once their optimal rotation is healthy.</p><p>"The best thing about Gabriela is she has the ability to impact the game in multiple ways without needing the ball," Marsh said. "Whether that's cutting hard and creating space for others, she's highly dependable."</p><p>No doubt about that. But you don't draft a player fifth just because they make the right cuts. Jaquez can do more. And with a shorthanded roster, they'll need her to be more sparkle than glue.</p><p>Vandersloot, Azurá Stevens, and DiJonai Carrington are all rehabbing injuries. There’s a wide open opportunity for Jaquez.</p><p>It's a chance to show that her NCAA title game performance — 21 points, 10 rebounds, 5 assists — is a preview of the player she can be in the pros.</p><p>Does that game feel like a long time ago, or like it was yesterday? Jaquez isn't sure.</p><p>A lot has happened since then.</p><p>She's been on Jimmy Kimmel, she's moved to Chicago, but on some level she feels like she's still in college.</p><p>She's waiting for some free time to establish her new life. Maybe get a car. Take a walk by the lake. Figure out who grown-up Gabriela is going to be.</p><p>In the meantime, she can keep being her usual self on the court.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module entries.title: Patriots coach Mike Vrabel addresses controversy about photos with NFL reporter
entries.summary: <p>FOXBOROUGH, Mass. — New England Patriots coach Mike Vrabel said Tuesday that he's had "difficult conversations with people I care about," including his family, his coaching staff, team officials and players, following the publication of photos of the coach and longtime NFL reporter Dianna Russini at an Arizona resort.</p><p>"Those (conversations) have been positive and productive. In order to be successful on and off the field, you have to make good decisions. That includes me. That starts with me," Vrabel said, making an unscheduled statement from the podium at the team's facility on the second day of its offseason workout program.</p><p>The photos were of Vrabel and Russini at a Sedona resort and were taken before the annual NFL meetings that began in Phoenix on March 29, according to the New York Post, which published the photos earlier this month.</p><p>A Patriots spokesman said there are no plans for other team officials to address the issue further.</p><p>The NFL, meanwhile, said last weekend that it is not investigating Vrabel's behavior. NFL spokesman Brian McCarthy confirmed to The Associated Press on Saturday that the league is not looking into the matter.</p><p>Vrabel and Russini, who are both married, released written statements to the Post after the publication of the story downplaying what the photos depict. But Russini resigned from The Athletic less than a week later, after the Post's report prompted an internal investigation at The New York Times-owned sports outlet.</p><p>Vrabel said he addressed players about the matter on Monday after they arrived for the start of the voluntary workout program. Two Patriots players were scheduled to be made available to reporters on Tuesday, but Vrabel said he wanted to speak before they did. He also said he didn't want the interest in the Post photos to take attention away from the NFL Draft, which begins Thursday.</p><p>Vrabel said any conversations he's had with team officials would stay private.</p><p>"We never want our actions to negatively affect the team. We never want to be the cause of a distraction," he said. "There are comments and questions that I've answered with the team and will keep those private to ourselves."</p><p>Russini joined The Athletic in 2023 after nearly a decade at ESPN, where she held various roles, including "SportsCenter" anchor, NFL analyst and insider. She hosted a podcast for The Athletic and made appearances on their video platform.</p><p>Until his appearance Tuesday, Vrabel hadn't spoken in a news conference setting with reporters since the owners' meetings.</p><p>Last year, before his first season as Patriots coach, he addressed reporters as part of the lead-up to the NFL draft. He opted not to do that last week, with only Patriots vice president of player personnel Eliot Wolf speaking at that news conference on April 13.</p><p>"Very involved. Business as usual," Wolf said when asked how much Vrabel had been involved with the team's draft process. "I'd say he's been in there with us this round probably a little more than he was in there last year. ... He's been in there. He's been contributing. He's watched a ton of the players."</p><p>Vrabel, 50, won three Super Bowls as a player with New England. He is preparing for his second season as coach of the Patriots. He led the team to a 14-3 finish last season, which ended with a Super Bowl loss to Seattle.</p><p>Vrabel said his focus going forward is football.</p><p>"I care deeply about this football team and excited to coach them. I also know that I'm going to attack each day with humility and focus," he said. "What I can promise you is that my family, this organization, the team, the staff, coaches and our fans, most importantly, will get the best version of me going forward. That's what I know and I'm excited to do that."<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Gov. Pritzker bans state workers from using insider information on prediction market apps like Kalshi
entries.summary: <p>Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker on Tuesday signed an executive order barring state employees from using insider information to bet on prediction market apps, joining six other states that have moved to either restrict or regulate the burgeoning betting field.</p><p>Users on apps like Kalshi and Polymarket are betting on everything from the results of elections to economic decisions or war outcomes — even what words President Donald Trump will say in speeches. The governor said he’s concerned about the lack of oversight, including bets on events in which state employees can influence the outcome.</p><p>“This opens the door to insider trading and abuse of confidential information,” the governor said in a statement provided to the Sun-Times. “While the Trump Administration continues to be riddled with stories of appointees looking to make a profit, Illinois is stepping up to ensure those who are serving the public, not their own personal financial gain.”</p><p>The governor’s office cited recent examples of inside trading, including highly accurate bets made in February regarding U.S. Israel strikes on Iran, an anonymous trader who earned more than $400,000 after placing large bets on the removal of Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro and a user who placed a $40,000 bet that Open AI would launch an AI web browser before the end of the month. Another example cited was a trader who placed a surge of bets on Taylor Swift’s engagement shortly before it was publicly announced and earned significant returns.</p><p>Illinois law already prohibits current or former elected or appointed state officials or employees from using confidential information to gain for themselves or others. But the new executive order strengthens those protections in response to the risks of the newly popular prediction market apps and event-based gambling.</p><p>Under the order, any Illinois state employee, officer, appointee or board member of any state agency is prohibited from using nonpublic information to bet on prediction markets or event-based contracts. They also can’t share or use nonpublic information to help any other person in participating, regardless of relationship, affiliation or whether that person profits.</p><p>U.S. Rep. Nikki Budzinski, D-Ill., and Rep. Adrian Smith, R-Neb., last month also introduced federal legislation that would ban members of Congress from using prediction market apps related to political events or policy decisions. It would also extend to dependents and spouses, senior staffers, political appointees, the president, vice president and all senior executive branch employees. </p><p>Last week, former Chicago Mayor Rahm Emanuel, who is also mulling a 2028 presidential bid, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/04/15/rahm-emanuel-online-betting-prediction-markets-tax-proposal-presidential-bid" target="_blank" >voiced support for a 10% federal tax on online sports betting and prediction markets</a> to boost revenue for science and technology. Emanuel has also said he supports a ban for federal employees. </p><p>California Gov. Gavin Newsom, one of several Democrats likely to run for president in 2028, issued a similar executive order last month. Newsom's executive order prohibits California state officials from using insider knowledge to profit, or assist another person in profiting, on prediction markets. Nevada, Connecticut, Arizona, Utah and Tennessee have also taken steps targeting prediction market apps. </p><p>Pritzker's executive order goes into effect immediately.</p>
entries.author: Tina Sfondeles

entries.title: Comunidad escolar y de fútbol queda conmocionada tras detención de estudiante y su mamá por ICE
entries.summary: <p>Mientras que Ricardo Navarrete, de 18 años, trabajaba arduamente para hacer realidad sus sueños en el fútbol, la mayoría de sus días se veían así: despertarse, ir a la escuela, ir a la práctica y repetir.</p><p>El adolescente y su madre, Liliana Navarrete, dejaron Colombia y llegaron a Chicago como solicitantes de asilo en 2022. Ricardo siguió persiguiendo su pasión de toda la vida por el fútbol, creyendo que este deporte podría ayudarlo a él y a su familia a tener éxito en su nuevo país y que tal vez algún día podría jugar profesionalmente.</p><p>Se inscribió en la escuela, encontró un club de fútbol local y se convirtió en un jugador destacado. </p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-640000" name="module-640000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Ahora, como estudiante de último año en Mather High School en el vecindario de West Ridge, Ricardo estaba listo para el siguiente nivel. <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DU83cLsklx4/?img_index=2" target="_blank" >Se comprometió a jugar</a> para el poderoso equipo de fútbol de la comunidad Truman College después de graduarse.</p><p>Pero los planes universitarios de Ricardo son inciertos después de que el Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) lo detuviera a él y a su mamá el mes pasado. Ricardo ha descrito a amigos y familiares haber sido retenido en habitaciones pequeñas con docenas de hombres y otras duras condiciones.</p><p>La detención ha dejado a la comunidad de Mather y a los compañeros de equipo de Ricardo en shock. Es un recordatorio de que, aunque la aplicación de la ley de inmigración a gran escala en Chicago ha disminuido, la campaña de deportaciones masivas del presidente Donald Trump aún afecta a familias y estudiantes en la ciudad.</p><p>Amigos, familiares, compañeros de equipo y miembros de la comunidad realizaron una manifestación para Ricardo y su mamá el jueves en West Ridge, pidiendo su liberación y el fin de la agresiva estrategia de deportación de la administración de Trump. Sostuvieron pancartas que decían “liberen a nuestro amigo” y “estamos contigo Ricardo”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure con su caso de asilo." 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a0bbeb7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+128/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F96%2Fac%2F8b2308894e4d8f1c1daf6d3d6339%2Fmatherseniordetained-260418-10.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Steven Navarrete habló el jueves en una manifestación en West Ridge sobre su hermano Ricardo y la detención de su mamá, ocurrida después de que acudieran a una presentación rutinaria relacionada con su caso de asilo.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“No merecen estar ahí”, dijo Steven Navarrete, el hermano mayor de Ricardo. “Han luchado por todo lo que tienen en este país y están siendo detenidos injustamente”.</p><p>Los funcionarios federales de inmigración detuvieron a Ricardo y a su mamá el 16 de marzo, después de que se presentaron a lo que creían que era un chequeo rutinario de su caso de asilo, según Kristy Morrow, quien ha ayudado a conectar a la familia con abogados y tiene un hijo que juega al fútbol en un club con Ricardo. También organizó una recaudación de fondos en línea para ayudar con los gastos legales.</p><p>Morrow dijo que Ricardo y su mamá recibieron un correo electrónico que les indicaba acudir a la corte de inmigración para firmar algunos documentos. Ellos llegaron con su documentación, pero fueron detenidos frente al edificio. Morrow lo llamó una “trampa”.</p><p>En un comunicado, un portavoz de ICE confirmó que Ricardo y su madre fueron detenidos el mes pasado y dijo que fueron aprehendidos “por estar ilegalmente en los Estados Unidos”.</p><p>Los abogados de la familia dijeron que Ricardo y Liliana tenían una solicitud de asilo activa cuando fueron detenidos y ninguno cuenta con antecedentes penales. La familia buscaba escapar de la violencia en Colombia, según amigos y familiares.</p><p>Grupos de derechos de inmigrantes han acusado a funcionarios federales de inmigración de emboscar a los inmigrantes cuando se presentan a audiencias judiciales, incluyendo a aquellos con solicitudes de asilo pendientes. Cientos de migrantes han sido arrestados mediante esta táctica.</p><p>La familia y los amigos dicen que ha sido difícil comunicarse con Ricardo y su mamá porque los funcionarios los han trasladado de un centro de detención a otro. En el último mes, Ricardo ha estado detenido en Oklahoma, Louisiana, Ohio e Indiana, aseguran. Actualmente, él y su mamá están siendo mantenidos en instalaciones separadas en Kentucky, según una base de datos de ICE.</p><p>Ese movimiento también dificulta obtener representación legal, ya que la familia necesita encontrar abogados autorizados para actuar en esos estados.</p><p>“Cada vez que lo trasladan a un nuevo lugar es como empezar de nuevo”, dijo Morrow.</p><div class="Enhancement" Su hijo forma parte del equipo de fútbol del club de Ricardo y ella ha trabajado para poner a la familia en contacto con recursos legales." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a0e0778/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4f%2Fe0%2Fb6049c5a407da4766b001db22645%2Fmatherseniordetained-260418-5.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9e57664/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4f%2Fe0%2Fb6049c5a407da4766b001db22645%2Fmatherseniordetained-260418-5.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a0e0778/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4f%2Fe0%2Fb6049c5a407da4766b001db22645%2Fmatherseniordetained-260418-5.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Kristy Morrow (a la derecha) asistió el jueves a la manifestación en apoyo a Ricardo. Su hijo forma parte del equipo de fútbol del club de Ricardo y ella ha trabajado para poner a la familia en contacto con recursos legales.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Los abogados presentaron peticiones de hábeas corpus la semana pasada desafiando la prisión de Ricardo y su mamá. Morrow dijo que tienen la esperanza de que las audiencias judiciales con un juez puedan ocurrir tan pronto como la próxima semana.</p><p>Ricardo ha contado a amigos y familiares que ha dormido en el suelo sin cobija y ha pasado tiempo en confinamiento solitario.</p><p>“Todas las instalaciones de detención de ICE operan en estricta conformidad con los estándares federales aplicables para la seguridad, la sanidad y el trato humano de acuerdo con los estándares nacionales de detención”, escribió un portavoz de ICE en un comunicado en respuesta a preguntas de Sun-Times sobre los reclamos de Ricardo. “De hecho, ICE tiene estándares de detención más altos que la mayoría de las prisiones estadounidenses que retienen a ciudadanos estadounidenses”.</p><p>Costel Serban, quien entrena el club de fútbol de Ricardo, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/sports/2025/07/23/west-ridge-soccer-club-iproskills-academy-costel-catalin-serban-sports" target="_blank" >iProSkills Academy</a>, dijo que a pesar de todo lo que ha sucedido, Ricardo sigue siendo resiliente y optimista. Ha hablado con Ricardo varias veces desde que el adolescente fue detenido.</p><p>“Es increíble para mí ver a un joven de 18 años despertarse cada mañana en una celda y seguir diciéndome ‘Coach, no te preocupes, soy fuerte y sé que mi familia está allá afuera’”, dijo Serban.</p><p>Serban notó esa determinación cuando se conocieron por primera vez, poco después de que Ricardo y su madre llegaran a Chicago. Ricardo encontró el club de fútbol de Serban en línea y, como no tenía dinero para el transporte, caminó varias millas para llegar hasta sus instalaciones en invierno. Serban recuerda que era un “día muy frío” y que se sintió “sorprendido” por la madurez de Ricardo.</p><p>Aunque Serban no sabía mucho español, aprendió sobre la familia de Ricardo y su travesía a Estados Unidos.</p><p>“La vida de Ricardo estaba amenazada en Colombia y vino aquí para comenzar una nueva vida, para comenzar una carrera”, dijo Serban.</p><p>Los nuevos compañeros de equipo de Ricardo se unieron a su familia, ayudándolos a pagar un motel, comida y ropa mientras buscaban un apartamento.</p><p>“Es un gran jugador, es muy divertido estar con él, es un chico divertidísimo”, dijo Miles Morrow, el hijo de Kristy.</p><p>Comentó que el vínculo que forjaron a lo largo de los años “era como tener un hermano”. Las prácticas y partidos del equipo no se han sentido igual desde que Ricardo fue detenido.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure amigo, sosteniendo un letrero que decía “Liberen a nuestro amigo”.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Mateo Abrego, uno de los amigos cercanos de Ricardo en Mather, dijo que los pasillos de la escuela también se sienten diferentes. Mateo mencionó que los compañeros de clase usan camisetas con el nombre de Ricardo para mostrar su apoyo.</p><p>“Ha sido realmente difícil en la escuela, la gente realmente lo quiere”, dijo Mateo. “Es desgarrador y ha sido muy difícil de procesar”.</p><p>El maestro Eriq Vázquez, que dirige el comité de santuario en Mather, dijo que Ricardo está en buen estado académico y que busca obtener el diploma, a pesar de haber faltado a clases mientras estuvo detenido.</p><p>“El personal de Mather hará todo lo posible para enmendar la situación de la mejor manera que puedan para tratar de asegurar su futuro”, dijo.</p><p>Mateo, que es estudiante de último año como Ricardo, dijo que su amigo debería estar haciendo planes de fin de año con sus compañeros de clase.</p><p>“Ahora mismo debería estar enfocado en el baile de graduación, en cosas así para los seniors, y no es justo que esté allá con ICE”, dijo. “No es justo para nada”.</p><p><i>Contribuyó: Sarah Karp </i></p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Sarah Karp, audio

entries.title: Praise God and pass the Ozempic
entries.summary: <p>Sure, I'm taking Ozempic. Aren't you? Isn't everybody?</p><p>OK, that's an exaggeration. There's also Zepbound and Wegovy and all those other drugs that belong to the GLP-1 class of weight-blasting tonics. Some folks take those instead (though really, just among us Ozempic users — we view those as cheap knockoffs, right? Like a restaurant serving Red Gold ketchup instead of Heinz. We've got the good stuff).</p><p>Thirty million Americans — 1 in 8 — take GLP-1 drugs, which not only curb your appetite so you can be a svelter, happier, more successful you, but seem to offer a wide and expanding range of positive results, from quieting the howl of addiction to healing brain trauma. According to the rapidly building data, taking such drugs can cut your risk of heart attack or stroke by 20%. I mentioned to a young person of my acquaintance that I was taking Ozempic, and he expressed an emotion not often heard when old people are cataloging their medicines: envy. Ozempic is supposed to keep you young, he said, wishing he could get some.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-ea0000" name="module-ea0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Opinion bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Opinion</h2></div> </div><p>I believe that ship has already sailed for me, though freezing the decline process at this point would be welcome.</p><p>All of this is relatively new. Ozempic received FDA approval in December 2017. Researchers are dancing as fast as they can, but if after 10 full years of use, Ozempic causes your head to tumble off your shoulders, then the joke will be on humanity, again. Remember another hugely popular drug that helps keep you thin, nicotine. People didn't figure out tobacco's lethality for 400 years after Europeans first embraced it. Millions still haven't.</p><p>Though given Ozempic's fat-busting abilities, we'll accept the occasional head bouncing down the sidewalk, giving it a quick soccer flick as we pass.</p><p>Despite dieting continually for the past half-century — I've counted more calories than stars in the known universe — I would have never sought out Ozempic had Type I diabetes not fried my pancreas and a doctor suggested I might try it. Technically, Ozempic is used for Type II diabetes, to help your not-dead pancreas produce insulin, which doesn't mean much if the organ is merely decorative (There's a fun online shop for Type I diabetes T-shirts and various gadgets called "<a class="Link" href="https://www.uselesspancreas.com/" target="_blank" >The Useless Pancreas.”</a>) But apparently mine is still quivering, kind of — I seem to have what some call Type 1.5; doctors tend to shrug and mumble when pressed for details — so a GLP-1 drug might do some good.</p><p>Or not. I've taken Ozempic for over a year, with no result whatsoever except a couple weeks of constipation when I first started, cleared up by mixing MiraLAX into my morning coffee. I haven't lost any weight at all, which happens in about 10% of people using Ozempic.</p><p>But I haven't gained any weight either. People who take insulin — and I inject from one to four shots a day — often put on the pounds. So in my case, nothing happening might be a sign of progress.</p><p>Two important aspects should be addressed before this topic is gratefully abandoned. First, this is another example of the insured/uninsured divide. Ozempic will run around $1,000 a month if you pay out-of-pocket. With my insurance, it's $0 a month. If it cost money — my money — I would not be so sanguine about whether it works or not. Though with effort, or accepting a knockoff, you can cut that by half or even three-quarters. I'd rather live in a country where everybody has health insurance than a country building condos on the moon.</p><p>Second, I wonder how a slimmer nation will view obesity. For a while, the notion of fat-shaming was up there in the holy writ of political correctness. I'd no sooner suggest someone was fat than fling a racial slur. Now that the utopia of universal slenderness is being approached, abrupt thinness might be seen as an undesirable state — there's already a term, "Ozempic face," for celebrities who are ridiculed for the sunken eyes and hollow cheeks that come from rapid weight loss (poor Katy Perry, the celebrity everybody loves to ridicule, is the poster girl for Ozempic face).</p><p>Myself, I weigh 40 pounds more than my adult low, graduating from college at 148, and 40 pounds less than my continually pounding back Jack-on-the-rocks high. Which seems a sweet spot to be content with until nature steps in with the final, irreversible weight-loss regimen.</p><p>Regarding the whole topic, I defer to a line from "Light on in the Kitchen," Ashley McBryde's warm country ode to parental wisdom and love: "There's more to life than being skinny/If you feel fat, it's mostly in your mind."</p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: Mike Tomlin will join NBC's 'Sunday Night Football' pregame show
entries.summary: <p>Mike Tomlin is going from the glare of an NFL sideline to the glare of television lights.</p><p>The former Pittsburgh Steelers coach will join NBC's "Sunday Night Football" coverage as a pregame analyst, two people briefed on the move told The Associated Press on Tuesday.</p><p>The people spoke on condition of anonymity because NBC has not announced the move. The Athletic was the first to report on Tomlin.</p><p>Tomlin stepped down in January after 19 seasons as Steelers coach and was the most sought after free agent by the networks. NBC had a studio opening as it is retooling its "Football Night in America" pregame show. Hall of Fame coach Tony Dungy announced last month that he was told he would not be back after 17 years.</p><p>Host Maria Taylor and former Cowboys coach Jason Garrett are expected to return. Devin McCourty's contract expired at the conclusion of last season, but NBC would like to re-sign him. Pro Football Talk's Mike Florio and fantasy sports expert Matthew Berry are also expected to continue their roles.</p><p>The futures of Jac Collinsworth, Rodney Harrison and Chris Simms remain undetermined.</p><p>NBC has had its main studio crew at its network sports headquarters in Stamford, Connecticut, with Harrison, Collinsworth and Dungy at the game locations in previous seasons, but NBC is considering having its entire studio show from the stadium.</p><p>The 54-year-old Tomlin won 193 regular-season games in Pittsburgh, tied with Hall of Famer Chuck Noll for the most victories in franchise history. He led the Steelers to an NFL title in his second season in 2008 when they beat the Arizona Cardinals in Super Bowl 43. Pittsburgh returned to the Super Bowl two years later, but lost to the Green Bay Packers.</p><p>Tomlin had limited postseason success after that. He had an 8-12 mark in the playoffs, including seven straight losses at the end of his tenure, all by double digits.</p><p>Pittsburgh won the AFC North last season, but were routed 30-6 by the Houston Texans in the wild-card round, which was the most lopsided home playoff loss in team history.</p><p>The bigger question for Tomlin is whether the analyst gig will be for one season only. He has two years left on a contract extension he signed in 2024, including a club option for 2027. The Steelers could seek compensation from a team if Tomlin sought to return before his contract expired.<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Departing IG targets City Council Office of Financial Analysis
entries.summary: <p>The $537,000-a-year office created in 2014 to advise the City Council on financial issues and avoid a repeat of the parking meter fiasco has failed to deliver on that mission, the city’s chief watchdog said Tuesday.</p><p>Days before concluding her four-year term, Inspector General Deborah Witzburg said a shortage of both adequate staff and financial information closely held by the mayor’s office prevents the Council's Office of Financial Analysis from helping the Council be the the “co-equal branch of government” it aspires to be.</p><p>In a budget rebellion not seen since "Council Wars" in the 1980’s, a majority of alderpersons led by conservative and moderate Democrats rejected Mayor Brandon Johnson’s corporate head tax and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/12/23/mayor-brandon-johnson-city-council-alternate-budget" >approved an alternative budget</a>, including several revenue-generating items the mayor’s adamantly opposed.</p><p>But Witzburg said the renegades would have been in an even better position to challenge Johnson if only their financial analysis office had been “equipped and positioned to do what it’s supposed to do" — provide the Council with “objective, independent financial analysis.”</p><p>“We are entering new territory where the City Council is asserting new, independent authority over the budget process. It can’t do that in a meaningful way without its own access to financial analysis,” Witzburg told the Sun-Times.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" alt="Chicago Inspector General Deborah Witzburg speaks at a City Club of Chicago luncheon at Maggiano’s Banquets last November." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/29f027e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3960x2222+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2Fa4%2F269c02774966bdc8dd6c3ddf2096%2Fwitzburg110623-19.jpg data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago Inspector General Deborah Witzburg’s latest report focuses on the Chicago City Council’s Office of Financial Analysis.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Jim Vondruska/Jim Vondruska/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>But the Council's financial analysis office, she added, "has never been equipped or positioned to do what it needs to do. It needs better and more independent access to data and it needs enough staff to do its job. It has a small number of employees and comparatively limited access to data.”</p><p>The IG’s farewell audit examined the period from 2015 through 2023. During that time, the financial analysis office budget authorized “either three or four” full-time employees. It now has a staff of five .</p><p>Witzburg is recommending a staffing analysis to identify how many people the financial office really needs — and also recommending that the office "get data directly" from other city departments, " rather than having it go through the mayor’s office.”</p><p>The audit further recommends that the office develop “better procedures to meet their reporting requirements” in a timely manner. As it stands now, reports are delivered “sometimes late, sometimes not at all,” the inspector general said.</p><p>“We find that those reports have been both not timely and not complete in terms of what they are required to report on and that those reports therefore have provided limited assistance to the City Council in its responsibility to make decisions about the city’s budget,” she said.</p><p>The Council Office of Financial Analysis responded to the audit by saying it hopes to add at least three full-time staffers in the short-term and has made “some progress” over the last three years in improving their access to data, but not enough.</p><p>The office was created in 2014 to provide aldermen with expert advice on fiscal issues.</p><p>For nearly two years the reform was stuck in the mud over whether former Ald. Helen Shiller (46th) had the independence and policy expertise to lead the office.</p><p>Shiller ultimately withdrew her name, but the office was a bust nevertheless. In an attempt to breathe new life into it, sponsors pushed through a series of changes.</p><p>Instead of allowing the Budget chair alone to request a financial analysis on a proposal impacting the city budget, any alderman was allowed to make that request.</p><p>The office was further required to produce activity reports quarterly, not just annually.</p><p></p><p>Now former-Budget Chair Pat Dowell (3rd) then chose Kenneth Williams Sr., a former  analyst for the office, as director and gave him the "autonomy" the ordinance demanded. </p><p>Two years ago, a bizarre stand-off developed in the office.</p><p>Budget Committee Chair Jason Ervin (28th) was empowered to dump Williams after Williams refused to leave to make way for a director of Ervin’s own choosing.</p><p>The stand-off began when Williams said he was summoned to Ervin’s office and told the newly-appointed Budget chair was “going in a different direction and I’m putting you on administrative leave” with pay.</p><p>“He took all my credentials and access away. I would love to come to work. I wasn’t allowed to come to work,” Williams said then. </p><p> Williams collected a paycheck for doing nothing while serving out the final days remainder of a four-year term.</p><p>Ervin’s resolution stated the director “may be removed at any time with or without cause by a two-thirds” vote or 34 alderpersons. He chose Janice Oda-Gray, who remains chief administrator.<br></p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Bears LB T.J. Edwards vows to be back 'when it's time to go' after breaking leg in playoffs
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> linebacker T.J. Edwards <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/01/11/bears-left-tackle-ozzy-trapilo-linebacker-t-j-edwards-out-for-season-after-injuries-in-packers-game" >broke his leg in the playoff win over the Packers</a> and isn’t on the field for the offseason training program, but said Tuesday his recovery is on track.</p><p>While Edwards didn’t want to put a timeline on his return, general manager Ryan Poles said in January the team expects he’ll be back by the start of training camp. The Bears will practice as part of Organized Team Activities starting next month.</p><p>“I will push as hard as I can to move that as quickly as possible; I also want to be smart about it,” Edwards said. “As hard as it is for me to not be out there every step of the way right now, I'm on a really good plan with the trainers and I'll be ready when it's time to go, for sure.”</p><p>Edwards, 29, has been an essential piece in the Bears’ rebuild and is under contract through next season. He led the team with 351 tackles over the last three seasons, in addition to seven sacks, five interceptions and four fumble recoveries.</p><p>Regarding any doubts about his ability to come back from the injury at this age, Edwards said, “I’ve been doubted my entire career, and that’s just fine. I thrive in that light.”</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: TE Colston Loveland was a surprise draft pick by Bears last year at No. 10, and now it's time for stardom
entries.summary: <p>Tight end Colston Loveland’s rookie season was good, but after <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >the Bears</a> traded their most accomplished wide receiver in DJ Moore, they need him to be great.</p><p>That was implied from the jump when the Bears drafted Loveland 10th overall last season, making him just the seventh player at that position to go in the top 10 this century. A tight end selected that high is expected to be a total game changer.</p><p>There were signs of Loveland’s potential to be that for the Bears last season, especially toward the end of it, but now it’s time for him to become the centerpiece of the passing game. They’re counting on wide receivers Rome Odunze and Luther Burden, too, but coach Ben Johnson made a big bet when pushing for the Bears to take Loveland a year ago.</p><p>“We’ve got guys that can go out there and make a play at any time, so ... maybe some games I take more or they take more some other games,” Loveland said Tuesday. “Just kind of play it by ear. The best thing is to always be ready.”</p><p>Loveland was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2025/04/24/bears-pull-stunner-at-no-10-drafting-michigan-te-colston-loveland" >a surprising pick last year</a> not only because Penn State’s Tyler Warren, who went 14th to the Colts, was generally ranked higher, but also the Bears already had Cole Kmet on a sizable contract. While Warren put up better numbers, Loveland quickly proved to be a good addition for the Bears.</p><p>He led them in receptions (58), yards receiving (713) and touchdown catches (six) last season despite finishing behind Odunze and Moore in targets. That included an average of 59.7 yards over the final 10 games.</p><p>He meshed well with Johnson’s offensive philosophy and demanding coaching style, too, which he credited for spurring his progress.</p><p>“He took a chance on me and asked me to do a lot of things,” Loveland told the Sun-Times in February. “With Coach Johnson, it’s never easy, but I always love when I’m almost intimidated by the head coach a little bit. ... If he needs to get on ass, he will, but that’s how we’d all want it.”</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true"
