entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Justin Fields pleased to get a new start with the Chiefs as Patrick Mahomes continues rehab
entries.summary: <p>KANSAS CITY, Mo. — Former Bears quarterback Justin Fields is ready for whatever role the Kansas City Chiefs need from him, whether that be starting for a few games while Patrick Mahomes is still recovering from knee surgery or serving as his backup once the regular season comes around.</p><p>The biggest thing is that his fourth organization in four years made the effort to acquire him.</p><p>The Bears traded Fields to the Pittsburgh Steelers after the 2023 season. Fields signed with the Jets as a free agent in 2025. </p><p>Earlier in the offseason, the Chiefs traded a sixth-round pick to the Jets and agreed to pick up $7 million of his guaranteed $10 million salary for the upcoming season. They needed insurance in the event Mahomes was not back by Week 1, and they also needed a backup quarterback with experience after Gardner Minshew struggled in that role with Kansas City last season.</p><p>"I was excited, excited for a new start," Fields said Tuesday in his first availability since the trade. "Of course there's tradition here, the culture here, the guys that are in the building, the experience — it was all I wanted. Definitely excited and excited to be here."</p><p>The 27-year-old Fields had signed a two-year, $40 million deal — with $30 million guaranteed — with New York last March. But he was just 2-7 as a starter, throwing for 1,259 yards with seven touchdowns and one interception. He threw for fewer than 55 yards on four occasions, including a season-low 27 yards in a loss to Buffalo in Week 2.</p><p>When the Jets traded for Geno Smith, it became clear that Fields would be moving on. New York was hopeful that it could find a trade partner, rather than simply releasing him, and general manager Darren Mougey found a taker in Kansas City.</p><p>"I appreciate Justin and the way he's gone about everything so far. He's been great with everything," Chiefs coach Andy Reid said. "I do not know him as well as I will have known him once we get through a few weeks here, but I like from afar what I've seen."</p><p>The Chiefs began the first phase of their offseason program Monday, but coaches are prohibited from on-field work with players.</p><p>"I mentioned down at the owner's meetings that we didn't bring him in to be a gadget guy, although he can do every gadget there is. But we didn't bring him in to do that," Reid said. "He's a legitimate quarterback, a starting quarterback in the National Football League, and we're lucky enough to have him here. And if that's the role that he plays early in the season, we have full confidence that he can do a great job with that. So far, I would tell you that's how I look at it."</p><p>Mahomes has made it clear he plans to be back on the field for Week 1. He's attending the voluntary offseason program in Kansas City, where he's been doing rehab work with the Chiefs' training staff, and has been seen around town at places such as the Big 12 men's basketball tournament.</p><p>He's also posted videos on social media of himself doing some heavy workouts.</p><p>"He goes to the meetings. He can lift, do all that. Rehab. That's the phase he's in right now," Reid said. "We'll just see. Kind of play it by ear, see where he's at. He's doing great. But we've just got to be smart with this thing."</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.author: AP

entries.title: NFL Mock Draft: Will the Bears take Blake Miller, Kayden McDonald or T.J. Parker at No. 25?
entries.summary: <p>For the first time under general manager Ryan Poles, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >the Bears</a> go into the draft Thursday targeting specific holes in the starting lineup rather than simply trying to stock a dilapidated roster with talent as they rebuild.</p><p>They need a starting left tackle and better production at defensive tackle and defensive end, making those the priorities in the first round at No. 25 and in the second at Nos. 57 and 60 overall.</p><p>They also need a starting safety, a long-term center and some reinforcements at cornerback and wide receiver, but it’s more likely they’ll attack those in the third round or later.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/halas-intrigue/2026/04/21/bears-nfl-draft-preview-podcast-halas-intrigue" >Halas Intrigue podcast: Bears draft preview</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>With the line of scrimmage front of mind, here’s a projection of the first round:</p><h3><b>1. Raiders — Indiana QB Fernando Mendoza</b></h3><p>There’s no guarantee Mendoza will be a star, but he lands in a solid situation with Tom Brady in ownership and top play caller in coach Klint Kubiak.</p><h3><b>2. Jets — Ohio State EDGE Arvell Reese</b></h3><h3></h3><p>The Jets are so far away that they’ll likely have a top pick next year to draft a quarterback, and Reese is widely considered the best overall talent in this draft.</p><h3><b>3. Cardinals — Texas Tech EDGE David Bailey</b></h3><p>Similar to the Jets, the Cardinals’ timeline doesn’t force them to force the issue at quarterback this year. It’s a multi-year path.</p><h3><b>4. Titans — Notre Dame RB Jeremiyah Love</b></h3><p>Love is the most exciting player in the draft and joins compelling team with last year’s top pick Cam Ward at quarterback.</p><h3><b>5. Giants — Miami OT Francis Mauigoa</b></h3><h3></h3><p>The Giants have their quarterback in Jaxson Dart and now must protect him after allowing the ninth-most sacks in the NFL last season.</p><h3><b>6. Browns — Ohio State WR Carnell Tate</b></h3><p>Does anyone know what the Browns are doing? Do they know? Unfortunately for Tate, he’s staying in-state.</p><h3><b>7. Commanders — USC WR Makai Lemon</b></h3><p>Lemon ends up in a better situation by going one pick later. He joins an ambitious organization and a talented quarterback in Jayden Daniels.</p><h3><b>8. Saints — Miami DE Rueben Bain</b></h3><h3></h3><p>Bain is an intriguing prospect for the Bears to track. Concerns about his character and arm length could cause him to slide.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/GaNVZJ8kLlE?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Bain of Your Existence | Rueben Bain NFL Draft Report & Scouting Profile"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p><h3><b>9. Chiefs — Arizona State WR Jordyn Tyson</b></h3><p>The Chiefs haven’t picked this high since 2013 and hope to get a receiver to pair with Patrick Mahomes for the second half of his career.</p><h3><b>10. Giants — Ohio State LB Sonny Styles</b></h3><p>Two picks in the top 10 give new coach John Harbaugh a chance to make an instant impact on the roster.</p><h3><b>11. Dolphins — Ohio State S Caleb Downs</b></h3><p>This could be the first steal of the draft. ESPN’s Mel Kiper has Downs going fifth to the Giants.</p><h3><b>12. Cowboys — LSU CB Mansoor Delane</b></h3><p>The Cowboys’ defense was disastrous last season, leading to coordinator Matt Eberflus’ firing, and especially bad against the pass.</p><h3><b>13. Rams — Utah OT Spencer Fano</b></h3><p>There’s a lot of debate about the best offensive tackle after Mauigoa, so the choices from here out will be subjective based on team preferences.</p><h3><b>14. Ravens — Penn State G Olaivavega Ioane</b></h3><p>Nothing wrong with fortifying either line of scrimmage, and the Ravens must strengthen the interior after losing Tyler Linderbaum.</p><h3><b>15. Buccaneers — Miami EDGE Akheem Mesidor</b></h3><p>The run on pass rushers begins here, likely taking some significant targets off the Bears’ board as they wait at No. 25.</p><h3><b>16. Jets — Tennessee CB Jermod McCoy</b></h3><p>McCoy would be the consensus best cornerback in the draft if not for missing last season to a torn ACL. He slides, but not far.</p><h3><b>17. Lions — Missouri EDGE Zion Young</b></h3><p>With Aidan Hutchinson signed through 2030, the Lions will be set for a long time if Young is a standout.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/JRKJ1BDywzs?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Forever Young 🙌 | Zion Young NFL Draft Report & Scouting Profile"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p><h3><b>18. Vikings — Clemson DT Peter Woods</b></h3><p>Woods and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/25/as-ohio-state-star-kayden-mcdonald-prepares-to-visit-bears-he-says-im-their-no-1-guy" >Ohio State’s Kayden McDonald</a>, both of interest to the Bears, are the top two defensive tackles in this draft class.</p><h3><b>19. Panthers — Alabama OT Kadyn Proctor</b></h3><p>Quarterback Bryce Young took an important step forward last season, and this is a good way to keep that progress going.</p><h3><b>20. Cowboys — Auburn EDGE Keldric Faulk</b></h3><p>After unloading Micah Parsons, the Cowboys now begin the search for a pass rusher who can make an impact like... Micah Parsons. Good luck.</p><h3><b>21. Steelers — Oregon TE Kenyon Sadiq</b></h3><p>The Steelers are hoping Aaron Rodgers returns, and this would give him a dynamic weapon underneath.</p><h3><b>22. Chargers — Texas A&M EDGE Cashius Howell</b></h3><p>Even in a supposedly weak draft for pass rushers, Howell would be the sixth off the board before the Bears’ pick.</p><h3><b>23. Eagles — Indiana WR Omar Cooper</b></h3><p>Drafting Cooper would be an ideal way to replenish at wide receiver with A.J. Brown likely getting traded to the Patriots.</p><h3><b>24. Browns — Georgia OT Monroe Freeling</b></h3><p>If Freeling lasts this long, it could compel the Bears to trade up for him. ESPN Scouts Inc. ranks him the No. 2 tackle this year.</p><h3><b>25. Bears — Clemson OT Blake Miller</b></h3><p>With uncertainty about Ozzy Trapilo’s recovery from a knee injury, the Bears need to make a long-term move at left tackle. If Trapilo bounces back and Miller pans out, they’ll have a surplus and a valuable trade piece. Ohio State defensive tackle Kayden McDonald and Clemson pass rusher T.J. Parker would be tempting here, but the Bears can’t go into the season with another free-for-all at left tackle.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/VjQqHtpyLzo?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="It's Miller Time 🍺 | Blake Miller NFL Draft Report & Scouting Profile"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p><h3><b>26. Bills — Clemson EDGE T.J. Parker</b></h3><p>The Bills are going to be fine offensively with former MVP quarterback Josh Allen, but need to add some punch to their pass rush.</p><h3><b>27. 49ers — Oregon S Dillon Thieneman</b></h3><p>Thieneman is better than the 27th overall player in the draft, but safety isn’t a premium position, which is why the Bears and other teams will address it in later rounds.</p><h3><b>28. Texans — Ohio State DT Kayden McDonald</b></h3><p>If the Bears go defensive tackle at No. 25, McDonald likely would be the choice. They’ve spent significant time with him, and he could be the best value pick in this draft.</p><h3><b>29. Chiefs — Clemson CB Avieon Terrell</b></h3><p>The Chiefs got this pick for sending cornerback Trent McDuffie to the Rams and must use it to replace him.</p><h3><b>30. Dolphins — Alabama QB Ty Simpson</b></h3><p>Malik Willis is on a relatively short-term contract, and Simpson could develop behind him for a season or two.</p><h3><b>31. Patriots — Washington WR Denzel Boston</b></h3><p>Even with Brown expected to come, the Patriots need to provide Drake Maye more at wide receiver.</p><h3><b>32. Seahawks — Notre Dame RB Jadarian Price</b></h3><p>Two Notre Dame running backs go in the first round as the Seahawks look to replace Super Bowl MVP Kenneth Walker.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore entries.title: Former Mayor Richard M. Daley recovering after suffering third stroke ahead of his 84th birthday
entries.summary: <p>Former Mayor Richard M. Daley, the longest-serving mayor in Chicago history, was hospitalized earlier this month after suffering what his brother described Tuesday as a third stroke.</p><p>Bill Daley said his older brother, who turns 84 on Friday, is “fine now.” He returned home after a few days in the hospital and is now undergoing rehab.</p><p>The younger Daley described the former mayor’s episode as a full-blown stroke, but a comparatively minor one compared to the serious stroke that Daley <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2014/7/9/18602742/judge-to-daley-lawyers-explain-why-ex-mayor-shouldn-t-testify" >suffered in 2014.</a> </p><p>“He had a stroke. He’s fine now. He’s home. He’s doing some rehab stuff, and that’s about it. ... That’s life,” said Bill Daley, who served as U.S. Commerce secretary under President Bill Clinton and White House chief of staff under former President Barack Obama.</p><p>“They said, he’s out of trouble. He has to do a little rehab. But he’s physically walking around doing everything and whatever. But you’ve got to watch yourself. [He's] not in any danger or anything, other than what everybody who’s 84 is in danger of.”</p><p>Bill Daley said there’s a big difference between his brother’s 2014 stroke and the comparatively minor one he suffered earlier this month.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-7c0000" name="image-7c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8df2d5b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2380x1336+0+126/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F67%2F3bde8eb24986ae7c8603b205bca5%2Fsoxopener-040916-31.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5723d6a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2380x1336+0+126/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F67%2F3bde8eb24986ae7c8603b205bca5%2Fsoxopener-040916-31.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fdb9b59/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2380x1336+0+126/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F67%2F3bde8eb24986ae7c8603b205bca5%2Fsoxopener-040916-31.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Former Mayor Richard M. Daley and his brother Bill Daley laugh as they wait for the start of the Chicago White Sox opening day game against the Cleveland Indians at U.S. Cellular Field April 8, 2016." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fdb9b59/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2380x1336+0+126/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F67%2F3bde8eb24986ae7c8603b205bca5%2Fsoxopener-040916-31.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/09fd19b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2380x1336+0+126/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F67%2F3bde8eb24986ae7c8603b205bca5%2Fsoxopener-040916-31.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fdb9b59/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2380x1336+0+126/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F67%2F3bde8eb24986ae7c8603b205bca5%2Fsoxopener-040916-31.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Former Chicago Mayor Richard M. Daley (right) and his brother Bill Daley laugh as they wait for the start of the White Sox Opening Day game against the Cleveland Indians at U.S. Cellular Field in 2016.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“I don’t know this doctor’s business, but usually, your first one is the most damaging. The first time, he was in for, God I don’t know, a couple of weeks. … He did rehab for a long time” in 2014, Bill Daley said.</p><p>“I talked to him the other day; he seemed to be fine, much better, obviously in no danger or anything. The fact that he was out [after this most recent stroke] in three days, and he was seeing visitors within hours doesn’t indicate to me that it’s serious” this time.</p><p>Bill Daley said the advice doctors have given the former mayor are the “things that everybody should do” if they want to live a long and vibrant life.</p><p>“Watch your diet. Keep your stress under control. All of that sort of stuff that they tell everybody who gets to a certain age. Lose weight. Keep your blood pressure down,” Bill Daley said. “Maybe he read one of your articles and got all excited.”</p><p>Bill Daley couldn’t resist making a playful joke when asked whether the former mayor’s speech was impaired.</p><p>“Not that I noticed. ... Some people thought his speech was impaired 40 years ago,” Bill Daley said, laughing.</p><p>The Daley brothers' father, former Mayor Richard J. Daley, suffered a serious stroke while in office in 1974, but returned to work and was elected to a sixth term in 1975. He died of a heart attack in December 1976.</p><p>Richard M. Daley has had a series of serious health problems.</p><p>In 2000, Daley was rushed to the hospital with what he thought was a heart attack. It turned out to be hypertension that close associates believed at the time was stress-induced. They said that the episode occurred the day the Chicago Sun-Times exposed how mayoral pal Oscar D'Angelo had engineered a deal to put two friends of Maggie Daley in business as part of a lucrative bookstore concession at O'Hare Airport.</p><p>Doctors disclosed then that, despite his family history, Daley had not had a physical in 10 years. </p><p>Fourteen years later, Daley suffered his most serious stroke. He had a less serious stroke a few years ago.</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Illinois businesses have mixed results with tariff refund process
entries.summary: <p>Illinois businesses report mixed experiences navigating the tariff refund process, after U.S. Customs and Border Protection launched an online portal Monday for firms to start filing claims.</p><p>Some companies submitted documents smoothly but are anticipating obstacles in getting refunded. Others are still trying to understand the complex guidelines before filing claims.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/economy/2026/02/20/supreme-court-trump-sweeping-tariffs-john-roberts-economic-agenda" target="_blank" >U.S. Supreme Court</a> in February ruled that some tariffs imposed by President Donald Trump last year were unconstitutional. Last month, the U.S. Court of International Trade determined that companies subjected to tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act were entitled to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/small-business/2026/03/05/illinois-businesses-tariff-refunds-federal-court-trade" target="_blank" >refunds</a>.</p><p>Toymaker Learning Resources of Vernon Hills, a plaintiff in the Supreme Court case, filed about 5,000 entries on Monday.</p><p>“The process went smoothly for us. We requested a refund in excess of $10 million,” Stephen Woldenberg, Learning Resources’ senior vice president, said. “We have other entries that we expect to submit at later stages in the refund process as well.”</p><p>Customs and Border Protection said in court filings that over 330,000 importers paid a total of about $166 billion on more than 53 million shipments, as of March 4.</p><p>Not all of those orders qualify for the first phase of the refund system’s rollout, which is limited to cases in which tariffs were estimated but not finalized or within 80 days of a final accounting.</p><p>To receive refunds, importers have to register for CBP's electronic payment system. As of April 14, 56,497 importers had completed registration and were eligible for refunds totaling $127 billion, including interest, the agency said.</p><p>Plastic Film Corp. in Shorewood makes swimming pool liners and other industrial materials at factories in Southeast Asia and North America. The company filed claims through its broker’s custom clearing office in a couple of hours on Monday morning.</p><p>“We've already been notified that all of our Phase 1 entries have been accepted,” Plastic Film Corp. CEO Joel Bittner said.</p><p>Plastic Film began working on its documentation in November, in anticipation of potential refunds while following the U.S. Supreme Court case. The company has 20 employees worldwide, with 14 in Illinois.</p><p>Bittner said this year’s court rulings give “reasonable expectations” of refunds.</p><p>“However, I am not hopeful that there won't be at least one attempt to block the return of the funds, either through the Trump administration appealing the international court ordering the refunds, or some other government intervention,” he said.</p><p>“As expected, [Monday's] refund portal launch is a mixed bag for small businesses,” Dan Anthony, executive director of national grassroots group We Pay the Tariffs, said. “Many in our coalition report challenges getting their online accounts in order to file. Among those who were ready to file [Monday], some reported a seamless process, while others received error messages and delays.”</p><p>St. Charles-based wholesale gift business The Winding Road imports goods from Nepal, Indonesia, Morocco, India, Peru and other countries. It sells to more than 3,500 retail stores across North America, Europe and Australia. Founded in 2011, the company has seven employees.</p><p>Owner Marla Showfer hasn’t yet applied for refunds and is still digesting “confusing instructions” on the government’s website. Winding Road’s customs broker, a large global logistics company, is charging $500 to file claims on their behalf; Showfer will likely hire it.</p><p>“I think it would take us too long to figure it out ourselves,” Showfer said. </p><p>Sari Wiaz, owner of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/small-business/2026/02/17/northbrook-small-business-baby-paper-national-campaign-tariffs" target="_blank" ><u>Baby Paper</u></a> in Northbrook, will file claims through her sourcing agent.</p><p>“She has told me that the process is complicated, and she therefore hired someone to guide her through the process,” Wiaz said. “We are all anxious to get our money back, but I am anticipating it to be longer than what is being conveyed, particularly for small importers that do not have the staff and resources.”</p><p>As they navigate the refund process, businesses are still weathering the impact of tariffs.</p><p>For Plastic Film, tariffs of about 60% have had “severe and lasting damage,” Bittner said.</p><p>“When the tariffs took effect, we were not given time to restructure our supply chain, renegotiate contracts or build adequate reserves. We were simply handed a dramatically higher cost of doing business overnight,” he said in a statement. </p><p>“To stay afloat, we were forced to do something we had worked hard to avoid: We borrowed money. … And with that debt came interest expenses that compound our burden month after month. We are now paying not only the tariffs themselves, but the ongoing cost of having had to finance them. Every dollar spent on interest is a dollar not spent on our people, our products or our future.”</p><p><i>Contributing: AP</i></p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/small-business/2026/03/05/illinois-businesses-tariff-refunds-federal-court-trade" >Illinois businesses press for tariff refunds after a federal court ruled in their favor</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Amy Yee

entries.title: Ex-Dolton Mayor Tiffany Henyard says she lived in Georgia while serving final days as Dolton mayor
entries.summary: <p>Former Dolton Mayor Tiffany Henyard told a county elections board in Georgia on Monday that she’s been living in that state since before her mayoral term ended last year — contradicting Illinois law.</p><p>Henyard, whose mayoral term was mired in controversy over <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/suburban-chicago/2025/01/27/dolton-mayor-tiffany-henyard-mismanaged-villages-finances-through-excessive-spending-lori-lightfoot-finds" >several misconduct investigations</a>, is running as a Republican for a Fulton County, Georgia, commissioner seat.</p><p>She had <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2025/02/25/dolton-mayoral-election-primary-tiffany-henyard-jason-house" >lost her reelection bid</a> for Dolton mayor in February 2025. The winner, Dolton Trustee Jason House, was sworn in as mayor May 5, 2025.</p><p>Henyard served as Dolton mayor through May 4, 2025, but she told the Fulton County Board of Elections that she has lived in Fulton County since May 1, 2025. Illinois law states mayors must live in the municipality they represent for their entire term.</p><p>“I lost the election in February. … I was no longer serving in that capacity,” Henyard said.</p><p>“Basically, I was a lame duck. Basically, I couldn’t do anything,” Henyard said, responding to questions from Commissioner Julie Adams.</p><p>“But you were mayor, correct?” Adams asked.</p><p>“My title was mayor, yes,” Henyard said.</p><p>“Did you know that in Cook County, Illinois, to hold an office there, you have to be a resident in that jurisdiction?” Adams said. “So, you were the mayor until May 4 of 2025, but yet you’re saying you became a resident of Georgia May 1 of 2025.”</p><p>“OK,” Henyard responded.</p><p>Henyard did not answer when questioned if she still was being paid by Dolton through May 4, the end of her term. Henyard also was a Thornton Township trustee at the time.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/bwBV8XFpO-M?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Fulton County Board of Registrations & Elections Special Called Meeting - April 20, 2026"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>The election board met Monday to question Henyard’s Fulton County residency because she’s required to live there for a full year before running for office. After about 30 minutes of questioning, the elections board voted 3-1 to approve Henyard’s residency requirement.</p><p>The investigations into Henyard’s conduct in Dolton include <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2024/06/14/dolton-tiffany-henyard-alex-nitchoff-oakk-construction-corruption-investigation" >an ongoing federal probe</a> into a $200,000 payment to a controversial contractor whose owner was convicted of bribery and sentenced to five years in federal prison.</p><p>Earlier this year, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/02/12/dolton-claims-fifth-third-bank-allowed-former-mayor-to-steal-1-9-million-in-public-funds" >the Village of Dolton sued Fifth Third Bank</a>, claiming the bank enabled Henyard to “steal” $1.9 million in public funds over at least one year.</p><p>Former Chicago Mayor Lori Lightfoot conducted a noncriminal investigation that found Henyard mishandled the village’s money through “excessive” spending with “very little oversight.” That probe examined payments to vendors without approval, which is the focus of the village’s recent lawsuit, as well as village employees’ trips to Las Vegas and Henyard’s use of federal relief funds through the American Rescue Plan Act.</p><p>Henyard also was accused in 2024 of attempting to cover up an alleged sexual assault of a township employee. In a lawsuit, the employee accused Andrew Holmes, a Dolton trustee and community activist, of raping her while in Las Vegas for a conference. The employee claimed Henyard tried to cover up the incident to protect herself and retaliated against the employee when she tried to come forward about the assault.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/editorials/2025/01/29/dolton-mayor-tiffany-henyard-financial-mismanagement-lori-lightfoot" >EDITORIAL: Dolton Mayor Tiffany Henyard’s big-spending ways get fully exposed</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: Billy Donovan goes, but abysmal run of Bull-y ball remains. Why would anyone bet on a change for the better?
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls" target="_blank" >Bulls</a> have no front office, having belatedly kicked executive vice president of basketball operations Arturas Karnisovas and general manager Marc Eversley to the curb.</p><p>They have no real roster, having thrown together a who’s-who of ‘‘who?’’ to go through the motions of another regular-season fade-out.</p><p>And now, with Billy Donovan having rescued himself from the abyss by <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/04/21/bulls-coach-billy-donovan-steps-away-six-seasons-helm" target="_blank" >resigning after six mostly lost seasons</a>, they have no coach.</p><p>But other than that?</p><p>Hey, they’re all set.</p><p>Sorry for the sarcasm, but it’s either that or the snooze button when it comes to considering the state of an organization that has been so baffling, blundering, bizarre and, worse still, so numbingly far from anything resembling relevance.</p><p>The NBA playoffs march on. So, in their own backward manner, do the once-proud Bulls.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/04/21/bulls-coach-billy-donovan-steps-away-six-seasons-helm" >Bulls coach Billy Donovan steps away after six seasons at helm</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/04/20/bulls-interview-process-candidates-for-new-vp-role" target="_blank" >Bulls have started interview process with candidates to lead front office</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Three consecutive seasons with fewer than 40 victories. Five out of six with a losing record and one playoff victory — a game, not a series — across the Donovan years. How much of that is on him, all, some or none, can be debated into eternity, as if it even matters. It feels as though it doesn’t because the Bulls have ceased to matter.</p><p>The Bulls haven’t won a playoff series since 2015, haven’t been to the Eastern Conference finals since 2011 and haven’t played for a championship since 1998. A devastating injury to Derrick Rose in 2012 accounts for some of it, maybe even a lot of it. But the mark of the Bull seems to be the overriding factor — ownership that isn’t committed enough to winning or principled enough about the right things, front offices with absurdly long leashes and de facto ‘‘stars’’ who aren’t nearly as good as the players around the league capable of hanging banners.</p><p></p><p>The mark of the Bull is strong. In the end, Donovan was powerless against it.</p><p></p><p>When he was introduced as coach in 2020, Donovan said of Karnisovas, ‘‘The thing that stood out to me was he wants a partner. He wants somebody to walk hand-in-hand with to help build things.’’</p><p></p><p>Alas, the Karnisovas regime turned out to be all thumbs.</p><p></p><p>Plan? What plan?</p><p></p><p>The Bulls certainly didn’t win enough. With their inimitable aimlessness, they also didn’t lose enough. Instead, they lived in the meaningless middle. What a waste of time.</p><p></p><p>Under Donovan, the Bulls won almost 47% of the time. Much better than they fared under Tim Floyd (.205), Bill Cartwright (.336) and Jim Boylen (.317). Roughly similar to the runs of Scott Skiles (.490), Vinny Del Negro (.500), and Fred Hoiberg (.426). Nowhere near Tom Thibodeau (.647), of course. A baseball lineup with those batting averages could do memorable damage.</p><p></p><p>Speaking of baseball, we’ve gone too long here without mentioning the ‘‘R’’-word. If Jerry Reinsdorf’s Bulls and White Sox swapped existences, would anyone be able to tell the difference? The mark of the Sox — who still haven’t won a playoff series since 2005 — is no joke, either.</p><p></p><p>Show of hands: Who’s betting on Team Reinsdorf to get this next Bulls chapter right?</p><p></p><p>Anyone?</p><p></p><p>Chirp, chirp, said the crickets.</p><p></p><p>We know it’s in the realm of possibility for any major pro team to find its way out of the wilderness. The Bears have done it even with a first-time general manager, a first-time head coach, a wet-behind-his-ears quarterback and the same, age-old ownership family. If the Bears could do it, the Bulls can do it.</p><p></p><p>You know, maybe.</p><p></p><p>Cue the crickets.</p><p></p><p>Donovan said in his farewell statement that his gratitude toward the Bulls is ‘‘permanent.’’</p><p></p><p>For his sake, one hopes the mark they’ve left on him is not.</p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true"
